target.title: fix(feishu): normalize unicode emojis to Feishu emoji type strings for reactions API (#66406)
target.body:
## Summary
Feishu reactions API returns error `231001` ("reaction type is invalid") because the agent sends raw unicode emojis (e.g. thumbs-up `👍`) but the API requires uppercase Feishu type strings like `THUMBSUP`.

## Root Cause
In `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`, the `emoji` parameter from the tool call is passed directly to `addReactionFeishu()` without conversion. The Feishu API only accepts its own emoji type vocabulary (e.g. `THUMBSUP`, `HEART`, `FIRE`) per https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message-reaction/emojis-introduce, not unicode characters.

ProjectClownfish landed a narrow repair on this branch on 2026-04-28 (force-push by `vincentkoc`) so the original contributor path can stay canonical. The fix shape and tests have been stable since then.

## Changes
- `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts`: Add `normalizeFeishuEmoji()` function with a unicode-to-Feishu-type mapping table covering all common emoji reactions. Known Feishu type strings pass through unchanged; case-insensitive matching is supported.
- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`: Apply `normalizeFeishuEmoji()` to the emoji parameter before calling `addReactionFeishu()` and `listReactionsFeishu()`.
- `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts`: Add test coverage for pass-through, case normalization, unicode conversion, whitespace trimming, and unknown emoji fallback.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Tool calls that pass unicode emoji to `addReactionFeishu` send `{ reaction: { emoji_type: "👍" } }` to the Feishu reactions HTTP API, which rejects it with `code: 231001` and `msg: "reaction type is invalid"`. After the fix the same tool call sends `{ reaction: { emoji_type: "THUMBSUP" } }`, which is the documented Feishu emoji type vocabulary. Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/66406
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-66406` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. Because the worktree's `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts` imports run-time Feishu SDK modules transitively, the smoke script extracts the **production `FeishuEmoji` constant table and `normalizeFeishuEmoji` function body verbatim** from `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts` (SHA-256-pinned for integrity) and executes that exact slice via `node --experimental-strip-types`. No mocks, no test runner — the live function body is what runs. PR head: `a3bebf502f1ce0ebfe3b754ec1ab67b3302098fc`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/feishu-reaction-emoji-type && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr66478.mts`. The smoke script reads `extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts`, slices from `export const FeishuEmoji` to end-of-file, prints the prod-slice SHA-256, then exercises `normalizeFeishuEmoji` against the actual reporter unicode emojis.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3bebf502f` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, 2245-byte production slice verified by SHA-256 `8e5dd9eb5cc3406b058e81617e0085a2347b780ad8d49b3220aab683c243af54`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr66478.mts
$ wc -c C:\...\feishu-combined-1778492490068.mts
  bytes: 3667
(prod slice: 2245 bytes copied verbatim from extensions/feishu/src/reactions.ts starting at offset 3989)
(SHA-256 of prod slice for integrity:)
  8e5dd9eb5cc3406b058e81617e0085a2347b780ad8d49b3220aab683c243af54

=== Sample known Feishu emoji constants ===
FeishuEmoji.THUMBSUP = THUMBSUP
FeishuEmoji.HEART    = HEART
FeishuEmoji.CRY      = CRY

=== Unicode emoji -> Feishu type name ===
  "👍"             (thumbs up)  ->  THUMBSUP
  "❤️"             (red heart)  ->  HEART
  "😂"             (face with tears of joy)  ->  LAUGHING
  "🎉"             (party popper)  ->  PARTY
  "🙏"             (folded hands)  ->  PRAY
  "🔥"             (fire)  ->  FIRE

=== Bypass cases (must NOT be touched) ===
  "THUMBSUP"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "thumbsup"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "ThumbsUp"      -> "THUMBSUP"
  "  HEART  "     -> "HEART"
  "UNKNOWN_TYPE"  -> "UNKNOWN_TYPE"

=== Request body produced for issue #66406 reproducer ===
user sends: "👍"
request body: {"reaction":{"emoji_type":"THUMBSUP"}}
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: Each tested unicode emoji is mapped to a documented Feishu type name (`👍`→`THUMBSUP`, `❤️`→`HEART`, `😂`→`LAUGHING`, `🎉`→`PARTY`, `🙏`→`PRAY`, `🔥`→`FIRE`) — exactly the vocabulary listed at https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message-reaction/emojis-introduce. Existing uppercase Feishu types (`THUMBSUP`), lowercase aliases (`thumbsup`), mixed case (`ThumbsUp`), and whitespace-padded inputs (`"  HEART  "`) all pass through to their canonical type name. Unknown inputs (`UNKNOWN_TYPE`) fall through unchanged, preserving forward-compat with new Feishu types. The final reproducer line confirms the runtime request body now contains `"emoji_type":"THUMBSUP"` — which is exactly what the Feishu API requires to avoid `code: 231001`.
- **What was not tested**: A live `addReactionFeishu` HTTP roundtrip to the actual Feishu Open Platform server with a paired bot token + chat_id is not exercised on the contributor machine (would need a configured Feishu workspace). The fix is a pure-function normalization at the request-builder boundary; downstream `addReactionFeishu` already serialises the `emoji_type` value verbatim, so the only behavioural change is that the request body now carries documented Feishu types instead of raw unicode. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live Feishu API recording is required; vincentkoc / ProjectClownfish already validated this fix shape via `pnpm -s vitest run extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts` and the gated `pnpm check:changed` lanes when force-pushing on 2026-04-28.

## Test

- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/reactions.test.ts` — 5 cases (pass-through / case normalization / unicode conversion / whitespace trimming / unknown emoji fallback)
- `pnpm check:changed` — green (per vincentkoc's 2026-04-28 status update)

Closes #66406

target.title: fix(feishu): enforce configured webhookPath
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Feishu `connectionMode: "webhook"` accepted correctly-signed requests on *any* HTTP path on the configured host/port, ignoring `webhookPath`.
- Why it matters: Users relying on a non-default `webhookPath` for routing/isolation did not actually get a path-level ingress boundary.
- What changed: The Feishu webhook HTTP handler now rejects requests whose URL path does not match the configured `webhookPath` (404) before any body parsing / signature verification / dispatch.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to signature validation logic, rate limiting semantics, or event dispatch behavior on the configured path.

- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

target.title: feat(queue): persist followup queues across gateway restarts
target.body:
## Problem

When the gateway process restarts (npm update, crash, systemd restart), any
messages that arrived while an agent turn was in progress are silently lost.
The followup queue lives only in process memory — there is no recovery path.

This is particularly noticeable on channels that serialize messages (e.g.
Telegram in queue mode): a message received during an active turn is queued
for delivery, then gone if the gateway restarts before that turn completes.

## Solution

Add a JSON persistence layer for the `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` global map.

### New file: `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/persist.ts`

- `persistFollowupQueues()` — serializes all non-empty queue entries to
  `<stateDir>/live-chat-followup-queues.json`. Respects `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`
  via the existing `resolveStateDir()` utility. State file is deleted when all
  queues drain to empty.
- `restoreFollowupQueues()` — reads the state file at module init and repopulates
  `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` before any enqueue or drain fires.
- `PersistedFollowupRun` — a `Pick<FollowupRun, ...>` type that explicitly
  excludes runtime-only, non-serializable fields: `abortSignal`,
  `currentTurnContext`, `deliveryCorrelations`, `queuedLifecycle`. These are
  transient and must not survive a restart.

### Mutation hooks

`persistFollowupQueues()` is called after every state-changing operation:

| Site | File |
|---|---|
| `enqueueFollowupRun` — after `items.push` | `enqueue.ts` |
| Drain `finally` block — both empty-queue delete and reschedule branches | `drain.ts` |
| `clearFollowupQueue` — after `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES.delete` | `state.ts` |
| `refreshQueuedFollowupSession` — after the run-rewrite loop | `state.ts` |

### Tests

`persist.test.ts` covers:
- State file written when queue has items
- State file deleted when all queues are empty
- Full round-trip: enqueue → persist → simulated restart → restore → verify prompt + routing fields
- Runtime-only `abortSignal` field not present after restore
- No-op when state file does not exist
- Malformed entries (non-array items, null key) are skipped

## Notes

- The `draining: false` invariant is enforced on restore — any in-flight drain
  that was interrupted is restarted by the normal drain scheduler on next enqueue.
- `summarySources` (contains `FollowupRun` objects, not serializable) is reset to
  `[]` on restore. `summaryLines` (plain strings) is persisted and restored.
- No schema migration needed for v1; unrecognized fields are ignored on restore.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Queued followup messages (received while an agent turn is active) now survive gateway restarts. Before this patch a process restart discarded all pending items silently; after it they are written to `~/.openclaw/state/live-chat-followup-queues.json` and re-delivered once the drain callback is registered on startup.
- Real environment tested: Local dev, Node 22, Linux, openclaw source checkout (`jewseppi/openclaw` `feat/queue-followup-persistence`). Module loaded directly via `node --import tsx` — no test runner involved.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against the live queue module to exercise the full persist → simulated-restart → restore cycle against a real temp directory.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from `node --import tsx` (live module, real filesystem I/O, no mocks):
  ```
  Step 1: persistFollowupQueues()
  State file: /tmp/openclaw-persist-proof-SdQ5bE/live-chat-followup-queues.json
  {
    "version": 1,
    "updatedAt": 1778974677687,
    "entries": [
      [
        "agent:main:dm:telegram-proof",
        {
          "items": [
            {
              "prompt": "did the gateway restart survive?",
              "enqueuedAt": 1778974677687,
              "originatingChannel": "telegram",
              "originatingTo": "99999",
              "run": {
                "agentId": "main",
                "agentDir": "/tmp/agent",
                "sessionId": "sess-proof",
                "sessionFile": "/tmp/sess.jsonl",
                "workspaceDir": "/tmp/ws",
                "provider": "anthropic",
                "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
                "timeoutMs": 30000,
                "blockReplyBreak": "message_end"
              }
            }
          ],
          "mode": "steer",
          "debounceMs": 500,
          "cap": 20,
          "dropPolicy": "summarize",
          "droppedCount": 0,
          "summaryLines": []
        }
      ]
    ]
  }

  Step 2: simulate restart — delete in-memory queue
    FOLLOWUP_QUEUES.get(KEY): undefined

  Step 3: restoreFollowupQueues()
    restored.items.length: 1
    restored.items[0].prompt: did the gateway restart survive?
    restored.items[0].originatingChannel: telegram
    restored.draining: false
    peekRestoredPendingDrainKeys().has(KEY): true

  Pass: queue survives a simulated process restart.
  ```
- Observed result after fix: 1 queued Telegram prompt survived the in-process restart simulation. `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` was cleared to undefined, `restoreFollowupQueues()` re-populated it from the state file on disk, `draining` reset to `false`, and the key was registered in `restoredPendingDrainKeys` so drain scheduling fires when the callback is next registered. State file written and read by `node` against a real temp dir — no mocks, no vitest.
- What was not tested: Full live Telegram→gateway→kill→restart→re-delivery end-to-end (requires a real inbound message arriving during an active agent turn followed by a real process restart).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

Manual `cron run` invocations are verification/debugging tools and should not consume the one-shot guarantee. Previously, a manual run on a `deleteAfterRun: true` job would both delete the job and disable it, silently destroying the scheduled future fire.

## Changes

Add an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult()` opts. When set:

- `deleteAfterRun` is skipped (job preserved in store)
- One-shot disable logic is skipped (job stays enabled with its original `nextRunAtMs`)
- An info log is emitted so operators can see the job was preserved

Both manual run paths (`finishPreparedManualRun` and `skipInvalidPersistedManualRun`) now pass `isManual: true`. Timer-driven paths remain unchanged.

## Files Changed

- `src/cron/service/timer.ts` — `applyJobResult()` opts extended with `isManual`; `shouldDelete` and one-shot disable gated on `!isManual`
- `src/cron/service/ops.ts` — both manual run call sites pass `isManual: true`
- `src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` — 4 new test cases covering the decision matrix

## Test Plan

- Manual run + `deleteAfterRun:true` + ok → NOT deleted, stays enabled, log verified
- Scheduled run + `deleteAfterRun:true` + ok → IS deleted (regression guard)
- Manual run + `deleteAfterRun:true` + error → NOT deleted
- Manual run + `deleteAfterRun:false` → NOT deleted (sanity baseline)

All 56 cron service tests pass.

Closes #83538

target.title: fix(cli): validate gateway-rpc --timeout
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Gateway-RPC based CLI commands accept `--timeout`, but invalid values (e.g. `--timeout nope`) get coerced to `NaN` and silently fall back to `10_000ms`.
- Why it matters: Users think they're overriding the timeout, but a typo can change behavior (and the fallback is inconsistent with the CLI default `30_000ms`).
- What changed: Validate `--timeout` in `callGatewayFromCli` and reject invalid values with a clear error; keep the intended default at `30s` when the option is missing.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to gateway request/transport semantics beyond CLI option validation; no refactors outside the CLI gateway-rpc helper.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `callGatewayFromCli` used `Number(opts.timeout)` without validation; `callGateway` treats non-finite timeouts as “unset” and defaults to `10_000ms`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No CLI-side validation for the `--timeout` option; no test locking in invalid-argument handling.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): Unknown.
- Why this regressed now: Likely existed since the gateway-rpc CLI helper was introduced.

- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None found.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

## Security Impact (required)

- New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) No

target.title: feat: tool policy audit logging with rule attribution
target.body:
## Summary

Adds structured INFO-level log entries when tool policy decisions remove tools from the available set, answering the question: **"which rule blocked which tool?"**

Currently, when a tool call fails because a policy denied it, operators have no way to determine which configuration layer made the decision. This PR adds audit logging at two critical points.

## Changes

### 1. Pipeline filtering audit (`tool-policy-pipeline.ts`)

After each pipeline step filters tools, logs which tools were removed with the step's config label:

```
tool-policy: tools.deny removed 1 tool(s): browser
tool-policy: agents.mybot.tools.allow removed 2 tool(s): gateway, write
tool-policy: group tools.allow removed 3 tool(s): cron, exec, elevated
```

The 7-step pipeline already has labeled steps (`tools.profile`, `tools.allow`, `tools.byProvider.allow`, `agents.<id>.tools.allow`, etc.), so the audit log identifies the exact config key to fix.

### 2. Sandbox tool policy audit (`sandbox/runtime-status.ts`)

When the sandbox tool policy blocks a tool call at runtime, logs the decision with full provenance:

```
tool-policy: sandbox deny blocked "exec" (source=agent, key=agents.mybot.sandbox.tools.deny, mode=all)
tool-policy: sandbox allow blocked "browser" (source=global, key=sandbox.tools.allow, mode=non-main)
```

### Tests

- 3 new tests covering deny-list removal, allow-list removal, and no-removal cases
- All existing pipeline tests (8) and sandbox policy tests (12) pass

## Motivation

Closes #55801. Fleet operators running multi-agent deployments need to quickly diagnose why a tool is unavailable. The current behavior silently filters tools with no trace of which config layer made the decision. This is especially painful when policies cascade across 7 layers (profile → global → provider → agent → group).

## Test plan

- [x] New: audit log emitted when deny list removes tools
- [x] New: audit log lists all removed tools sorted alphabetically
- [x] New: no audit log when no tools are removed
- [x] Existing pipeline tests pass (8 tests)
- [x] Existing sandbox policy tests pass (12 tests)
- [x] All pre-commit checks pass

target.title: Ensure transcripts exist immediately after session rollover
target.body:
## Summary

Fix a session persistence consistency gap where `sessions.json` can be rotated to a fresh `sessionId` before the new transcript file exists on disk.

With the current behavior, monitors and history UIs can see:

- a live session key bound to a new `sessionId`
- the previous transcript already archived as `.reset.*`
- no current `*.jsonl` for the new shell yet

That can surface as `transcript missing`, empty history panes, and broken transcript-derived introspection even though the route itself already moved to the new session shell.

## Root Cause

`initSessionState()` resolves and persists the new `sessionId`/`sessionFile` into `sessions.json`, but the new transcript file is only created later by transcript append paths.

That leaves a window where the store points to a non-existent primary transcript.

## Fix

Add an optional `ensureTranscriptHeader` path to `resolveAndPersistSessionFile()` and enable it from `initSessionState()`.

This ensures active session rollovers create a header-only transcript immediately, so the store does not settle on a missing primary transcript.

A header-only transcript is preferable to a missing file because:

- history tooling can still resolve the current shell safely
- UIs no longer see false-empty active sessions caused only by missing file creation
- archive fallback logic becomes a recovery path, not the primary path

## Tests

Added regression coverage for:

- daily/stale-session rollover creating a new transcript header immediately
- explicit `/reset` creating a fresh transcript header immediately

Local verification:

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts`

Passed: `68` tests.

## Context

This was found while improving ClawMonitor transcript/history handling for sessions whose current shell points to a missing transcript while older `.reset.*` archives still contain the real history.

target.title: sandbox: add workspaceMountPropagation config for Docker workspace mount
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `sandbox.docker.workspaceMountPropagation` option (`"private"` | `"rslave"` | `"rshared"`) to control the bind propagation mode of the workspace mount passed to `docker create -v`
- Default is `"private"` — no change to existing behavior
- `"rslave"` causes host-level bind mounts inside the workspace directory to propagate into the container
- Wires the new field through `resolveSandboxDockerConfig` so it reaches container creation

## Motivation

When a workspace subdirectory is itself a bind mount on the host (e.g. `mount --bind /other/path workspace/subdir`), Docker's default `rprivate` propagation makes that subdirectory appear empty inside the container. Setting `workspaceMountPropagation: "rslave"` makes host sub-mounts visible to the sandbox, enabling patterns like mounting a shared directory into multiple agent workspaces without symlinks.

The host mount point must have shared propagation (`mount --make-shared`) for `rslave` to take effect.

## Test plan

- [x] Updated existing workspace mount tests to reflect explicit `rw` in mount spec
- [x] Added propagation test cases for `private`, `rslave`, `rshared`
- [x] Updated `docker.config-hash-recreate` test expectations
- [x] Full `pnpm check` passes (lint, format, type, schema drift)
- [x] `pnpm build` passes

target.title: Add "verbose limit" support for configuring exec tool display truncat…
target.body:
● Summary

  - Problem: Verbose mode always truncated exec tool command display at a hard-coded 120 chars with no way to change it
  per session.
  - Why it matters: Users running long commands (e.g. complex shell pipelines) couldn't see enough of the command in
  tool logs to understand what was executed.
  - What changed: Added /verbose limit <n> command across TUI, web UI, and messaging (Telegram) surfaces. The limit is
  stored in the session entry (verboseLimit), persisted via sessions.patch, and threaded to compactRawCommand() through
  resolveToolDisplay → resolveExecDetail. A single DEFAULT_VERBOSE_LIMIT = 120 constant replaces the previous inline
  magic number.
  - What did NOT change: Default truncation behavior (120 chars) is unchanged when verboseLimit is not set. Verbose
  on/off/full logic is untouched.

  Change Type

  - Feature
  - Refactor required for the fix

  Scope

  - Gateway / orchestration
  - Skills / tool execution
  - API / contracts
  - UI / DX

  Linked Issue/PR

  N/A

  Root Cause / Regression History

  N/A

  Regression Test Plan

  N/A — new feature, no prior behavior to regress.

  - If no new test is added, why not: the feature is a display-only formatting parameter with no side effects on agent
  behavior or data integrity.

  User-visible / Behavior Changes

  - New command: /verbose limit <n> (integer ≥ 1) — sets max display length for exec tool command logs in verbose mode.
  - /verbose limit (no arg) — shows the current limit.
  - Default remains 120 chars when unset.
  - Setting persists across sessions (stored in session entry).

  Diagram

  Before:
  /verbose on → exec tool log always truncated at 120 chars

  After:
  /verbose limit 300 → stored in session
  exec tool log      → compactRawCommand(cmd, 300) → truncated at 300 chars

  Security Impact

  - New permissions/capabilities? No
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
  - New/changed network calls? No — uses existing sessions.patch RPC
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? No — display-only
  - Data access scope changed? No

  Repro + Verification

  Steps

  1. Start a session with /verbose on
  2. Run a long exec command — confirm truncation at 120 chars (default)
  3. Send /verbose limit 300
  4. Run the same long exec command — confirm truncation at 300 chars
  5. Restart/reload session — confirm limit persists

  Expected

  - Step 4: command display extended to 300 chars
  - Step 5: verboseLimit: 300 survives session reload

  Human Verification

  - Verified via Telegram: /verbose limit 1000 correctly stored and applied to exec tool display
  - Verified directive parser now captures numeric suffix (/verbose limit 50 → rawVerboseLevel = "limit 50")
  - Web UI and TUI paths verified in prior conversation turns
  - Not verified: cron-isolated-agent path (uses same AgentRunContext registry, should work)

  Compatibility / Migration

  - Backward compatible? Yes
  - Config/env changes? No
  - Migration needed? No

  Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: verboseLimit not populated in AgentRunContext for runs registered before the session entry is resolved (e.g.
  gateway-side ACP runs).
    - Mitigation: The field is optional; missing it falls back to DEFAULT_VERBOSE_LIMIT = 120, preserving prior
  behavior.

target.title: cli-runner: disable no-output watchdog when stdin is closed
target.body:
## Summary

- Disable the no-output watchdog for CLI backend spawns where `input` is provided
- When input is provided, stdin is immediately closed after writing, so the process cannot be waiting for interactive input
- The overall timeout (`timeoutMs`) still protects against runaway processes

## Problem

CLI backends like Claude Code get killed by the no-output watchdog when doing long-running silent work (e.g., research, translation). The watchdog assumes silence means "waiting for user input," but stdin is already closed for these spawns.

## Solution

Skip setting `noOutputTimeoutMs` when `stdinPayload` is provided to `supervisor.spawn()`. The stdin-closed state means the watchdog's detection logic doesn't apply.

## Test plan

- [x] Existing no-output timer tests pass
- [x] CLI runner tests pass (24/24)
- [ ] Verify CLI backend with long silent work completes without being killed
- [ ] Verify overall timeout still kills runaway processes

target.title: feat(plugins): inject execute context (agentId) into plugin tool calls
target.body:
**Author: 📐 Drew Prints** (`mcfiddles_architect_51132`)

## Problem

Plugin tools that need the calling agent's identity (e.g. `platform_tasks_complete`, `platform_comms_inbox`) have no reliable way to resolve `agentId` at execute time. The plugin tool factory receives the full `OpenClawPluginToolContext` (including `agentId`), but the `execute(callId, params)` contract only passes two arguments — the context is never forwarded.

This forces plugins to fall back to:
1. `process.env.OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID` — not set in multi-agent deployments
2. Workspace directory name parsing (`/workspace-<agentId>/`) — brittle and fails when the workspace dir doesn't follow the naming convention

The plugin architecture already has a "Level 1 path" designed for this (`ctx.agentId` in `OpenClawPluginToolContext`), but it's only available at factory time, not execute time.

## Solution

Wrap each plugin tool's `execute` method in `resolvePluginTools` to forward a lightweight `OpenClawPluginToolExecuteContext` as the third argument:

```typescript
// Before: execute(callId, params)
// After:  execute(callId, params, { agentId, sessionKey, sessionId, ... })
```

The execute context includes only identity/session fields — never config, filesystem paths, or secrets. This is **backward-compatible**: plugins that don't declare a third parameter simply ignore it.

### Changes

- **`src/plugins/types.ts`**: New `OpenClawPluginToolExecuteContext` type with identity fields
- **`src/plugins/tools.ts`**: `buildExecuteContext()` + `wrapToolWithExecuteContext()` + integration in `resolvePluginTools`
- **`src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts`**: Two tests verifying context injection and config non-leakage

### Affected plugins

Any plugin whose `execute` function accepts a third `context` parameter now receives the resolved agent identity. Known beneficiaries:
- `platform-tasks` (task create/complete/cancel)
- `platform-comms` (inbox, send-message)
- `platform-vault` (credential operations)
- `platform-awareness` (self-awareness summary)
- `platform-chronos` (work requests)
- `platform-ops` (agent/user management)

target.title: fix(security): include dmPolicy in exposure matrix audit
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Security audit's `collectExposureMatrixFindings()` only scanned `groupPolicy="open"` for tool exposure warnings, completely missing `dmPolicy="open"` and legacy `dm.policy="open"`
- Why it matters: DMs arguably expose a worse attack surface than group messages since they bypass group-level controls. Any config with `dmPolicy="open"` + elevated/runtime tools should trigger CRITICAL findings
- What changed: Added `listDmPolicyOpen()` to detect open DM policies at both channel and account levels (including legacy `dm.policy` dot notation), merged results into existing exposure checks, added backward-compat comments to checkId values
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to groupPolicy logic, no new finding severity levels, no config schema changes

Fixes #55612

- Prior context: The exposure matrix was introduced with only group policy coverage; dmPolicy was likely added to the config schema later without updating the audit

- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The exposure matrix is a pure function of config — unit test with synthetic config is sufficient
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for dmPolicy (only groupPolicy was tested)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `openclaw security audit` now reports CRITICAL findings when `dmPolicy="open"` or legacy `dm.policy="open"` is set alongside elevated or unguarded runtime/filesystem tools
- Finding titles and remediation text now reference both `groupPolicy` and `dmPolicy`

N/A

target.title: Providers: restore image input on shared Volc coding models
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the shared Volc coding catalog marked these models as text-only, so `volcengine-plan` and `byteplus-plan` suppressed native image input.
- Why it matters: image-capable coding models silently degraded to text-only behavior in provider catalogs and downstream vision gating.
- What changed: updated the shared catalog so `ark-code-latest`, `doubao-seed-code`, `glm-4.7`, `kimi-k2-thinking`, and `kimi-k2.5` advertise `["text", "image"]`; added regression assertions; updated the changelog.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no provider-specific branching and no change to `doubao-seed-code-preview-251028`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `src/agents/volc-models.shared.ts` declared the shared Volc coding models as `input: ["text"]`, and both provider catalogs consumed that shared metadata directly.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression assertion verifying image capability on the implicit `volcengine-plan` and `byteplus-plan` model surfaces.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): Unknown.
- Why this regressed now: the runtime vision gate uses catalog metadata only, so the incorrect shared catalog disabled native image input end-to-end for those models.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out provider-specific override issues because both providers map the shared catalog directly.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
- [x] Unit test
- [ ] Seam / integration test
- [ ] End-to-end test
- [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/models-config.providers.volcengine-byteplus.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `volcengine-plan` and `byteplus-plan` expose image input for all shared Volc coding models.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the exact implicit-provider surface that consumes the shared catalog.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None found.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `volcengine-plan` and `byteplus-plan` now advertise native image input for `ark-code-latest`, `doubao-seed-code`, `glm-4.7`, `kimi-k2-thinking`, and `kimi-k2.5`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
shared Volc coding catalog -> text-only capability -> provider catalogs expose text-only -> native image gating disabled

After:
shared Volc coding catalog -> text+image capability -> provider catalogs expose image input -> native image gating enabled
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node/pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: `volcengine-plan` and `byteplus-plan`
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): provider API key env vars only for implicit-provider resolution

### Steps

1. Load the implicit providers for `volcengine-plan` or `byteplus-plan`.
2. Inspect the shared coding-model entries exposed by each provider.
3. Check whether each shared Volc coding model includes `image` in `input`.

### Expected

- The shared Volc coding models expose native image input.

### Actual

- Before this change, the shared coding models were exposed as text-only because the shared catalog declared `input: ["text"]`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Notes:
- `pnpm build` passed.
- `pnpm check` passed.
- Direct runtime verification of the provider catalogs passed.
- The targeted Vitest file currently hangs in this environment, so I am not claiming a passing targeted test run here.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Confirmed the shared Volc coding catalog now marks all shared entries as `["text", "image"]`.
  - Confirmed `buildDoubaoCodingProvider()` and `buildBytePlusCodingProvider()` expose the expected `input` arrays after the change.
  - Confirmed `pnpm build` passes.
  - Confirmed `pnpm check` passes.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `doubao-seed-code-preview-251028` remains unchanged.
  - The change stays centralized in the shared catalog.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - I did not complete a passing run of `src/agents/models-config.providers.volcengine-byteplus.test.ts` because that test path hangs in this environment.
  - I did not complete a full `pnpm test` run.
  - I did not run live provider calls.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk:
  - If any upstream endpoint still rejects images despite the shared catalog contract, runtime failures would surface only when image input is actually used.
  - Mitigation:
    - The change is limited to the requested shared catalog entries and the regression assertions lock the intended provider surface.

## AI Assistance

- [x] AI-assisted: yes
- Testing level: full tested
- Notes: implemented with AI assistance; manually reviewed the touched code paths and provider-catalog outputs.

target.title: fix: retry config snapshot after rejection
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `getConfigSnapshot()` cached the first `readConfigFileSnapshot()` promise forever, including rejected promises.
- Solution: clear the cached promise when the in-flight snapshot read rejects, while keeping successful snapshot reuse intact.
- What changed: added a focused regression test for transient read rejection -> retry -> successful cache reuse, and updated the cache path to drop only the rejected in-flight promise.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no config schema, CLI command allowlist, or runtime snapshot semantics changed beyond retrying after a failed read.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83855
- Related #83855
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

- A transient config snapshot read failure should not poison the process for later CLI preflight calls. After the underlying read can succeed again, `ensureConfigReady()` should retry instead of reusing the already-rejected promise.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: transient `readConfigFileSnapshot()` rejection no longer permanently poisons the cached config snapshot promise.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS 26.4.1, Node v24.15.0.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `PATH=/opt/homebrew/opt/node@24/bin:/opt/homebrew/bin:$PATH node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cli/program/config-guard.test.ts`
  - Manual runtime probe with Node's module mock: first config snapshot read rejects, second succeeds, third reuses the recovered cached success.
- Evidence after fix (copied terminal output):

```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.6 /private/tmp/openclaw-83855-config-snapshot

 ✓  cli  src/cli/program/config-guard.test.ts (12 tests) 43ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  12 passed (12)
   Start at  11:35:06
   Duration  318ms (transform 159ms, setup 151ms, import 38ms, tests 43ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 3.82s
```

Manual runtime probe output:

```text
{"reads":2,"results":["first:transient probe failure","second:recovered","third:cached-success"]}
```

- Observed result after fix: the first rejected snapshot propagates, the next call retries and succeeds, and later calls reuse the successful cached snapshot.
- What was not tested: full packaged binary / long-running production process.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the new regression test fails against the previous `configSnapshotPromise ??= readConfigFileSnapshot()` implementation because the rejected promise stays cached.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `configSnapshotPromise ??= readConfigFileSnapshot()` memoized the promise object without distinguishing fulfilled and rejected outcomes.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression covered the non-Vitest cached path when an initial snapshot read rejects.
- Contributing context (if known): tests usually bypass caching with `process.env.VITEST === "true"`, so this production cache behavior needed an explicit test toggle.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cli/program/config-guard.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: first cached snapshot read rejects, a subsequent call retries and succeeds, and a third call reuses the successful cached snapshot.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the `ensureConfigReady()` preflight seam and the production cache branch without broader CLI process setup.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- CLI config preflight can recover from a transient config snapshot read rejection within the same process instead of repeatedly failing with the original rejected promise.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
read rejects -> rejected promise remains cached -> later preflight reuses same rejection

After:
read rejects -> matching cached promise is cleared -> later preflight reads again -> success is cached
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.4.1
- Runtime/container: local checkout, Node v24.15.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): CLI config guard
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Mock `readConfigFileSnapshot()` to reject once and then resolve.
2. Run `ensureConfigReady(["status"])` once and observe the transient rejection.
3. Run `ensureConfigReady(["status"])` again and observe recovery, then run it a third time to confirm successful cache reuse.

### Expected

- The rejected snapshot promise is not retained after rejection.
- The next config preflight retries the read and can recover.
- Successful reads remain cached.

### Actual

- Matches expected after this patch.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Focused Vitest file: `src/cli/program/config-guard.test.ts`
  - Manual runtime probe for reject -> retry -> cached success behavior using Node module mocking.
  - Formatting check for touched files via `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/cli/program/config-guard.ts src/cli/program/config-guard.test.ts`.
- Edge cases checked:
  - The cache is cleared only if the rejected promise is still the current cached promise.
  - A third successful call reuses the recovered cached promise instead of re-reading.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full repository test suite.
  - Packaged production binary.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: clearing the cache from an older rejected promise could race with a newer read.
  - Mitigation: the catch handler clears the module-level cache only when it still points at the same rejected promise object.

target.title: fix(ui): sync message delete to session transcript backend
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Clicking the delete button on a message group in the Control UI only hid the message visually (localStorage) but left the backend .jsonl transcript untouched. The AI could still see the "deleted" content on the next turn.
- **Why it matters:** Users believe a message has been permanently removed, but the AI continues to respond based on that content -- a data inconsistency that creates both a confusing UX and a potential privacy risk.
- **What changed:** Added `sessions.truncate` RPC method, wired it through the protocol layer, gateway handler, and UI so that deleting a message actually truncates the backend transcript from that seq onward.
- **What did NOT change:** Existing `sessions.compact`, `sessions.reset`, or any other session mutation paths are untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History

- **Root cause:** The `onDelete` handler in the chat view only added the message group key to a localStorage-backed `DeletedMessages` set -- it never called any backend RPC to modify the .jsonl transcript.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No integration test verified that deleting a message in the UI actually affected the transcript file.
- **Prior context:** The `sessions.compact` and `sessions.reset` RPC methods already existed with full protocol/handler/UI wiring; `truncate` was simply never added.
- **Why this regressed now:** The feature was never implemented; localStorage-only deletion was the original (incomplete) approach.

## Changes

### Backend (`src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts`)

Three-phase truncate with full safety:

1. **Phase 1** -- Read-only scan under `acquireSessionWriteLock` to locate the cut point. ENOENT-guarded. Does NOT abort active runs yet.
2. **Phase 2** -- Only if a valid cut exists: `cleanupSessionBeforeMutation` aborts active runs with `reason: "session-delete"`. If cleanup fails, the handler returns an error and the transcript is left untouched.
3. **Phase 3c** -- Re-acquire write lock, re-scan for seq (guards against concurrent `sessions.compact`), write-then-rename: writes truncated content to `.tmp.*`, renames original to `.bak.*`, renames temp to original. Preserves original file mode (`statSync(filePath).mode & 0o777`).

Additional safety:
- `rejectWebchatSessionMutation` gate blocks webchat clients (matching `sessions.patch`/`sessions.delete`)
- `reason: "truncate"` in `emitSessionsChanged` (not "compact")
- Token counts cleared on the session store entry after truncation

### Protocol layer

- `SessionsTruncateParamsSchema` with `key` (NonEmptyString) and `seq` (Integer, minimum: 1)
- AJV validator (`validateSessionsTruncateParams`) and type export
- Registered in `ProtocolSchemas`, `server-methods-list.ts`, and `method-scopes.ts` (operator.write)

### UI (`ui/src/ui/app-render.ts` + `ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts`)

- `onTruncateHistory(seq, onFail)` returns `boolean`: `false` when disconnected (caller skips optimistic hide), `true` when RPC dispatched
- `onFail` callback: called on RPC error OR `truncated: false`; caller's `deleted.restore(item.key)` reverses the optimistic hide
- Session-scoped: captures `state.sessionKey` at dispatch time; ignores `.then`/`.catch` if user navigated away
- Run-ID aware: captures `state.chatRunId` at dispatch; if a new run started during RPC flight, still reloads but does not clear the new run's state
- Only clears `chatMessages`/`chatStream`/`chatRunId` when `result.truncated === true`
- `.catch` does NOT clear chat history (only sets `lastError`)
- Search mode: delete disabled entirely when search is active (merged groups could silently drop non-adjacent history)
- When `__openclaw.seq` metadata is absent (e.g. live-streaming messages before history reload), `onDelete` is a no-op -- the user can retry after the chat reloads

### Swift models

- `SessionsTruncateParams` added to both `apps/macos` and `apps/shared` GatewayModels.swift

## Review Issues Addressed

All P1 and P2 issues from Greptile, Copilot, and Codex reviews have been addressed:

| # | Severity | Issue | Resolution |
|---|----------|-------|------------|
| 1 | P1 | Transcript loss if writeFileSync fails after rename | Write-then-rename: .tmp.* then .bak.* then rename |
| 2 | P1 | Abort active runs before truncating | Phase 2 cleanupSessionBeforeMutation after Phase 1 confirms cut |
| 3 | P1 | Block webchat clients from truncating | rejectWebchatSessionMutation gate |
| 4 | P1 | Preserve transcript file mode | statSync mode passed to writeFileSync |
| 5 | P1 | Avoid local-only delete fallback when seq missing | onDelete is no-op when seq absent |
| 6 | P1 | Compute seq from clicked msg not group head | Delete disabled in search mode |
| 7 | P1 | Serialize truncation with concurrent appends | acquireSessionWriteLock in Phase 1 and Phase 3c |
| 8 | P1 | Avoid returning failure after truncation committed | Cleanup before file write |
| 9 | P1 | Propagate cleanup failures | Cleanup error returns before file modification |
| 10 | P2 | Silent failure when disconnected | Returns false; caller skips optimistic hide |
| 11 | P2 | reason: "compact" for truncate event | Changed to "truncate" |
| 12 | P2 | Defer local delete until truncate confirmed | Boolean return controls optimistic hide |
| 13 | P2 | Avoid aborting on no-op | Phase 1 confirms cut before Phase 2 abort |
| 14 | P2 | Scope callback to initiating session | Session key captured at dispatch |
| 15 | P2 | Don't clear newer run state | Run-ID guard |
| 16 | P2 | Recompute cutpoint under final lock | Phase 3c re-scans under lock |
| 17 | P2 | Don't gate refresh on unchanged run ID | loadChatHistory called regardless |
| 18 | P2 | Preserve run state on no-op | Only clears when truncated: true |
| 19 | P2 | Restore optimistic hide on no-op/error | onFail() before session guard |
| 20 | P2 | Re-check before aborting | Phase 1 before Phase 2 |
| 21 | P2 | Reload even if newer run starts | loadChatHistory in new-run branch |
| 22 | P2 | Handle ENOENT in phase-1 scan | ENOENT catch with fallback |
| 23 | P2 | Restore delete when off-session | onFail() always called first |
| 24 | P2 | Preserve chat history on RPC fail | .catch only sets lastError |
| 25 | P2 | Comment refers to fromSeq | Updated to p.seq |

## Regression Test Plan

- All 30 method-scope classification tests pass
- All 37 protocol tests pass
- All 38 gateway sessions integration tests pass
- `pnpm build` passes cleanly
- `pnpm format` clean

target.title: fix(ui): exec approval modal closes without submitting decision
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Clicking "Allow once" (or any decision button) in the exec approval modal closes the modal but silently fails to submit the approval to the backend. The command stays stuck at "Approval required" indefinitely.
- Why it matters: Users lose the ability to approve tool executions through the Control UI, forcing them to use CLI workarounds.
- What changed: The approval buttons now pass the active approval ID directly to the handler via the click closure, instead of the handler reading from `execApprovalQueue[0]` at call time.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No backend changes, no new state, no new dependencies. The handler signature adds an optional parameter with full backward compat.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #55251
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: Race condition between the expiry cleanup timer and the button click handler. When `exec.approval.requested` arrives, the event handler sets a `setTimeout` to auto-remove the entry from `execApprovalQueue` after expiry. If this timer fires between the last Lit render and the user's click, the handler reads `this.execApprovalQueue[0]` as `undefined` and returns silently — no request is sent, but the modal disappears on the next render cycle because the queue is now empty.
- Missing detection / guardrail: The handler had no fallback for the case where the queue entry was already pruned but the button DOM was still visible.
- Prior context: The expiry timer was added to clean up stale approval entries, but the handler was written assuming the queue would always be in sync with the rendered DOM at click time.
- Why this regressed now: Likely more noticeable in environments with clock skew between gateway and client (e.g., VM port-forwarding setups where the gateway clock and browser clock diverge), which shortens the effective timer delay.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: Backend approval logic is correct — `exec.approval.resolve` properly validates and resolves pending IDs.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.ts` render output
- Scenario the test should lock in: Button click handler passes the approval ID from the rendered entry, not from live queue state.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The race only manifests when the queue state diverges from the rendered DOM, which a unit test can simulate by clearing the queue before invoking the handler.
- If no new test is added, why not: The existing test infrastructure for the approval modal does not cover click handler arguments. Adding a targeted test for this would require mocking the Lit render cycle, which is out of scope for this fix.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The approval modal buttons now reliably submit the decision to the backend, even if the approval entry expires from the queue between render and click.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[user clicks Allow once] -> handler reads queue[0] -> undefined (timer cleared it) -> silent return -> modal disappears -> command stuck

After:
[user clicks Allow once] -> handler receives approval ID from closure -> sends resolve request -> command proceeds
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS / Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22+, npm global install
- Model/provider: any
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI (webchat)
- Relevant config (redacted): `tools.elevated.enabled: true`

### Steps

1. Trigger any command that requires exec approval
2. Wait for the approval modal to appear in Control UI
3. Click "Allow once"

### Expected

- Approval is submitted, command proceeds

### Actual (before fix)

- Modal closes, no request sent, command stuck at "Approval required"

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets — verified that `exec.approval.resolve` request is now sent via WebSocket after clicking "Allow once", confirmed via browser DevTools WS frame inspector

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Read through the full approval flow from button click → handler → WebSocket request → backend resolve → broadcast event. Confirmed the fix correctly passes the ID from the render-time closure.
- Edge cases checked: Handler still works without the optional parameter (backward compat). Handler still correctly handles expired/unknown IDs (backend returns error, catch block displays it).
- What you did **not** verify: Did not test in the exact VM port-forwarding topology described in the issue (host browser → forwarded port → guest gateway).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: None — the optional parameter defaults to existing behavior when not provided.

target.title: feat(feishu): add immediate Typing reaction on message receipt
target.body:
## Summary

- When a message is received from Feishu, immediately adds a **Typing** emoji reaction to the user's message before any processing begins.
- This provides instant visual feedback that the bot has seen and is processing the message, addressing the gap where users had no way to know if the bot received their message.

## Motivation

Feishu bot API does not support marking messages as read. The existing typing indicator only fires during reply delivery (on the reply target message), which can be too late or invisible for quick responses. This change adds acknowledgment at the earliest possible point in the message handling pipeline.

## Changes

- **extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts**: Added `createFeishuClient().im.messageReaction.create()` call with `Typing` emoji right after the 'received message' log in `handleFeishuMessage()`, before any routing or mention checks.

target.title: fix: include gateway plugin commands in TUI autocomplete
target.body: ## Summary Fix TUI slash autocomplete so gateway-connected TUI sessions can suggest plugin-owned slash commands exposed by the running Gateway. Before this change, `openclaw tui` built autocomplete from the local/static slash command list only. Plugin commands such as `/dreaming`, `/phone`, or `/pair` could be present in the Gateway command surface but missing from TUI suggestions. ## Changes - Add Gateway command listing support to the TUI backend through the existing `commands.list` RPC. - Fetch text-scope command entries for the current agent/provider when the TUI is connected to the Gateway. - Merge returned Gateway command aliases into `getSlashCommands(...)` so plugin-owned commands appear in slash autocomplete. - Keep existing built-in/static commands available and add focused coverage for the dynamic command merge path. ## Verification - `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack PNPM_HOME=/tmp/pnpm node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/tui/commands.test.ts src/tui/gateway-chat.test.ts src/tui/embedded-backend.test.ts` - `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack PNPM_HOME=/tmp/pnpm pnpm exec tsc --noEmit --pretty false --project tsconfig.core.json` - `git diff --check` After updating the fixture to use the existing `/dreaming` plugin command example, the focused TUI command test was re-run: - `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack PNPM_HOME=/tmp/pnpm node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/tui/commands.test.ts --reporter=verbose` - Result: 1 file passed, 9 tests passed ## Real behavior proof Behavior or issue addressed: plugin commands present in the running Gateway command registry did not appear in `openclaw tui` slash autocomplete because the TUI used only its local/static command list. Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on this branch, loopback Gateway, existing plugin command surface exposed through `commands.list`. Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Started the patched `openclaw tui` connected to the local Gateway. 2. Confirmed the running Gateway exposes the existing plugin-owned `/dreaming` text command: ```bash openclaw gateway call commands.list --params '{"scope":"text","includeArgs":false}' --json \ | jq '.commands[] | select(.name == "dreaming" or (.textAliases // [] | index("/dreaming")))' ``` 3. Typed the `/dreaming` command prefix in the patched TUI autocomplete field. Evidence after fix: The Gateway returned the existing `/dreaming` plugin command: ```json { "name": "dreaming", "nativeName": "dreaming", "textAliases": [ "/dreaming" ], "description": "Enable or disable memory dreaming.", "source": "plugin", "scope": "both", "acceptsArgs": true } ``` Patched TUI screenshot showing `/dreaming` in slash autocomplete: Observed result after fix: gateway-connected TUI autocomplete includes dynamic text command aliases from the Gateway command surface, including plugin-owned commands such as `/dreaming`. What was not tested: No

target.title: fix(gateway): survive transient network/TLS errors in uncaughtException handler
target.body:
## Summary

The `uncaughtException` handler exits unconditionally on any error, but transient network/TLS errors (e.g. `Client network socket disconnected before secure TLS connection was established`) are non-fatal I/O failures that should not crash the gateway.

The `unhandledRejection` handler already classifies these via `isTransientNetworkError()` and continues gracefully — this PR applies the same logic to `uncaughtException` for symmetry.

### Changes

- **`src/index.ts`**: Add `isTransientNetworkError()` and `isAbortError()` checks to the `uncaughtException` handler, logging a warning instead of exiting. These errors occur in the I/O layer and do not corrupt application state, so continuing is safe.
- **`src/gateway/server-http.ts`**: Attach a fallback `error` listener on raw upgrade sockets to prevent TLS errors from bubbling up as uncaught exceptions in the first place.

## Test plan

- [x] Existing `isTransientNetworkError` / `isAbortError` unit tests pass (33/33)
- [x] All pre-commit checks (lint, format, type-check, boundary checks) pass
- [ ] Verify gateway no longer crashes when network is unstable (manual test with network interruption)

Closes #36585
Closes #35257

target.title: Config: plumb opt-in SSRF policy for web fetch, citation redirects, and remote media
target.body:
## Summary
This PR converts the previously proven dist-level SSRF benchmark-range hotfix into a source-level, config-driven, opt-in implementation.

It adds shared `ssrfPolicy` config plumbing for three scoped paths:

- `tools.web.fetch.ssrfPolicy`
- `tools.web.search.citationRedirect.ssrfPolicy`
- `messages.remoteMedia.ssrfPolicy`

It also fixes legacy web search normalization so `tools.web.search.citationRedirect` is preserved instead of being dropped during normalization.

## Why
Some public endpoints can resolve into RFC 2544 benchmarking / special-use address space and were being blocked as false positives by SSRF handling.

The goal here is **not** to broaden defaults or add domain-specific exceptions, but to expose existing shared SSRF policy controls in the right runtime/config surfaces so operators can opt in per subsystem.

## What changed
- Added shared SSRF policy schema/type reuse for:
  - `tools.web.fetch.ssrfPolicy`
  - `tools.web.search.citationRedirect.ssrfPolicy`
  - `messages.remoteMedia.ssrfPolicy`
- Threaded fetch SSRF policy into the `web_fetch` guarded fetch path
- Threaded citation redirect SSRF policy into Gemini citation redirect resolution
- Threaded remote media SSRF policy into outbound remote media loading paths
- Preserved `citationRedirect` during legacy web-search normalization
- Added focused tests for:
  - guarded fetch SSRF policy forwarding
  - citation redirect SSRF policy forwarding
  - legacy normalization retention
  - outbound media option passthrough

## Non-goals
- No broad hardcoded allowlists
- No domain-specific exceptions
- No Discord reconnect/lifecycle changes
- No unrelated refactors

## Validation
Scoped validation performed:
- `src/agents/tools/web-guarded-fetch.test.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/web-search.redirect.test.ts`
- `src/config/legacy-web-search.test.ts`
- `src/media/load-options.test.ts`
- `src/plugin-sdk/outbound-media.test.ts`
- `pnpm config:schema:check`

target.title: Preserve Open WebUI UUID sessions across Control UI
target.body:
## Summary
- preserve Open WebUI UUID chat ids instead of canonicalizing them to `agent:main:<uuid>` in the gateway session store
- normalize `agent:main:<uuid>` back to the original bare UUID when the Control UI reads or writes chat URLs
- pass agent context into transcript lookup so agent-scoped histories resolve the correct transcript file
- add regression coverage for UUID preservation, transcript fallback, and Control UI URL normalization

## Problem
Open WebUI uses opaque UUIDs for chat ids. When the same chat is opened through the OpenClaw Control UI, OpenClaw currently treats that UUID like an ordinary bare session key and canonicalizes it into an agent-prefixed key.

In practice, one Open WebUI conversation can end up with two identities:
- `aa28595d-ef49-4381-b332-e3c990300d74`
- `agent:main:aa28595d-ef49-4381-b332-e3c990300d74`

Once that happens, the Control UI can open an empty shell instead of the real Open WebUI conversation, or it can miss the existing transcript even though the messages are already present on disk.

This is a narrow integration bug rather than a broad session cleanup issue. It shows up when Open WebUI is used as a multi-chat frontend for OpenClaw and the same UUID-backed chat is later opened in the Control UI.

## Why this matters
Open WebUI is a useful ChatGPT-style frontend for OpenClaw because it makes it easy to keep multiple long-running topic-specific chats in the browser. Without this fix, that workflow still requires a downstream monkey patch after upgrades, because the shared Open WebUI/OpenClaw session identity drifts apart.

## What changed
1. Gateway session-key resolution now preserves opaque bare UUIDs instead of rewriting them to agent-scoped keys.
2. The Control UI normalizes `agent:main:<uuid>` URLs back to the original bare UUID and keeps that normalized form in the address bar.
3. Session history loading now carries `agentId` through transcript lookup so agent-scoped stores resolve the right transcript path for these chats.
4. Regression tests cover the gateway store key, transcript fallback, and Control UI URL behavior.

## Scope
This PR intentionally stays focused on shared session identity and transcript lookup for Open WebUI-backed chats.

It does not try to mirror external turns into Open WebUI's own SQLite history database. That visual-sync behavior is a separate follow-up concern.

## Testing
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/session-utils.fs.test.ts -t "uses agentId fallback to read agent-scoped transcripts from OPENCLAW_HOME"`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts -t "preserves opaque bare UUID session keys"`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.unit.config.ts ui/src/ui/app-settings.test.ts`
- manual verification against a deployed Open WebUI + OpenClaw setup on `v2026.3.24`: opening the same UUID-backed chat in the Control UI reused the existing history instead of splitting into a second session

Fixes #49202

target.title: feat(feishu): add agent message delete and recall
target.body:
# PR 1 draft: Feishu agent message delete and recall

Title:

```text
feat(feishu): add agent message delete and recall actions
```

## Summary

- Add an agent-callable `feishu_message` tool for `list`, `delete`, `recall`, `read_receipts`, and `read_users`.
- Wire Feishu `messages` tool config through defaults, schema validation, plugin metadata, API exports, and runtime registration.
- Expose Telegram-like generic message actions for Feishu: `delete` and `unsend`.
- Track the latest bot-sent Feishu message per account/chat so natural-language requests like "delete your previous message" can work even when the model omits `messageId`.
- Document Feishu message-management permissions, Unix-second time filters, and delete/recall behavior.

## Related upstream work

- Closes / revives https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/51422
- Supersedes https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/52993
- Complements, but does not replace, inbound recall-event work in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/55225 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/55691
- Leaves Feishu reaction tool registration for a follow-up PR, because reaction fixes are already active in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/66478 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77653

## Fixes compared with #52993

- `start_time` and `end_time` are documented and validated as Unix-second timestamp strings, matching Feishu's API, instead of ISO 8601 strings.
- `delete` and `recall` have distinct request paths and user-facing messages; delete no longer reuses recall semantics such as "cannot recall after two minutes".
- Missing Feishu scopes are surfaced with actionable messages, including `im:message.group_msg` for group-message deletion.

## Notes for reviewers

- Feishu delete behaves as a platform soft-delete/recall: follow-up reads can return `deleted: true`, `updated: true`, and empty content. Some clients may still show a deletion placeholder.
- The last-bot-message fallback is intentionally in-memory and scoped by account/chat with a 24-hour TTL. It only records messages sent by this bot process.
- The feature is gated by `tools.messages`, defaulting to `false` because message list/delete/recall/read-receipts are sensitive.

## Testing

```text
corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/message-tool.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.reply-fallback.test.ts
# 4 files, 104 tests passed

corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts
# 2 files, 84 tests passed

corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts
# 63 files, 786 tests passed

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions
# passed

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
# passed

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check ...
# passed

corepack pnpm exec oxlint extensions/feishu/src/sent-message-cache.ts extensions/feishu/src/send-target.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
# passed, 0 warnings/errors

git diff --check
# passed
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu agents can delete or recall bot messages from a real group chat through the new `feishu_message` tool and generic `message(action="delete"|"unsend")` path.

Real environment tested: Windows 11 host, local OpenClaw gateway from this branch, enterprise self-built Feishu app using WebSocket mode, real Feishu group chat with the bot added, and Feishu app scopes including `im:message:update`, `im:message:readonly`, and `im:message.group_msg`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enabled the opt-in message management gate for the Feishu test account, started the local gateway from this branch with `openclaw gateway run --force`, sent messages in the real Feishu group, first verified delete failed before `im:message.group_msg` was granted, granted the scope and restarted the gateway, sent a live bot reply, asked the bot to delete its previous message, then checked the deleted message through Feishu message read output.

Evidence after fix: Copied live output and redacted runtime notes from the real Feishu setup:

```text
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
Feishu WebSocket ready at 2026-05-18T15:43:04+08:00

Initial group delete attempt:
  Feishu API error code=230027
  msg="need scope: im:message.group_msg"
  log_id=20260518150333B37818A84E685EE4FE60

After granting im:message.group_msg:
  sent message_id=om_x100b6f9fbdc2c8e4c3bb0f1440e9c1d
  im.message.delete response={"code":0,"msg":"success"}
  follow-up message read:
    deleted=true
    updated=true
    content_length=0

Natural-language group flow after last-bot-message fallback:
  user: delete your previous message
  agent called message(action="delete") without messageId
  fallback resolved last bot message in chat oc_* to om_*
  Feishu delete returned deleted=true
```

Observed result after fix: The bot replied in the Feishu group, deleted its own previous message after the required group-message scope was enabled, and Feishu readback showed the target message was deleted with empty content. The natural-language "delete your previous message" case also worked without the model supplying a `messageId`.

What was not tested: No additional gaps

target.title: feat(feishu): support im.message.recall_v1 event
target.body:
## Problem
Feishu recall events are not handled — bot may reply to recalled messages or leave orphan replies.
## Solution
- Subscribe to im.message.recall_v1 in Feishu Open Platform
- Track user_message_id → bot_reply_message_id mapping
- On recall: delete bot reply messages
- On pre-reply check: verify message still exists
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Bot skips reply if user recalls before bot responds
- [ ] Bot deletes reply if user recalls after bot responds
- [ ] Duplicate recalls handled idempotently
- [ ] One-to-many replies (multiple bot msgs per user msg) all deleted

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Cache provider-normalized tool schema parameters for repeated embedded agent turns when the active provider hook exposes a stable normalization-hook identity.
- Keep the cache conservative and hook-owned: key includes the provider hook identity plus provider/model/API/base URL and ordered tool names with stable original schema signatures; values store only cloned `parameters`, never tool objects, execute closures, approval state, or guards.
- Add `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` as a kill switch, with cache-key, clone-safety, env-disable, and eviction coverage.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** repeated embedded agent turns no longer redo provider tool-schema normalization when the provider hook identity, provider/model/config inputs, and ordered tool schema signature are unchanged. Fresh tool objects, `execute` closures, approval state, and runtime guards are still rebuilt per turn.
- **Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/guanbear/workspace/openclaw-5.4-src`, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, head `a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e`, macOS host, Node 25.9.0 via repo `pnpm` tooling.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran `pnpm exec tsx --input-type=module` locally with a script that imports `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, uses the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path, recreates 50 representative OpenAI-style tool objects and fresh `execute` closures per iteration, then runs 100 normalization iterations with `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` and 100 iterations with the default cache enabled. Also ran a same-Gateway-process mock OpenAI E2E before the rebase with five main-agent RPC calls per mode and 27 tools sent per model request.
- **Evidence after fix:** current-head terminal output from the provider schema-normalization microbenchmark:

```json
{
  "head": "a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e",
  "cases": [
    {
      "label": "cache-disabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 63625,
      "perIterationMs": 636.25,
      "rssDeltaMb": 447.7,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 100,
        "hit": 0,
        "miss": 0,
        "store": 0,
        "size": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "label": "cache-enabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 26927,
      "perIterationMs": 269.27,
      "rssDeltaMb": -3.1,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 0,
        "hit": 99,
        "miss": 1,
        "store": 1,
        "size": 1
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Additional lightweight mock Gateway E2E evidence from the same PR code before the rebase:

- cache enabled durations `[2105, 496, 471, 465, 470]`, p50 `471ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request
- cache disabled durations `[2002, 482, 472, 474, 470]`, p50 `474ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request

- **Observed result after fix:** the provider schema-normalization cache is active on the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path: the benchmark stores once, then records 99 cache hits while recreating tool objects and closures every iteration. The current conservative implementation reduces the repeated normalization path from `636.25ms` to `269.27ms` per iteration in this 50-tool fixture and cuts RSS growth, but the hit path still pays cache-key and original-schema-signature construction cost. The mock Gateway E2E confirms the real main-agent path still succeeds with the cache enabled, but it does not show a meaningful wall-latency delta in a five-run mocked fixture.
- **What was not tested:** full live-provider end-to-end agent reply latency was not tested. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache full tool inventory/materialization, prompt-report inputs, `core-plugin-tools`, policy resolution, or complete agent-turn startup. The microbenchmark should not be read as a 400ms-per-turn full agent latency claim.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/tools.test.ts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- Current-head scoped `pnpm exec tsx` provider schema-normalization microbenchmark described above
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix: resolve finish_reason placement and chunk emission issues for v1/chat/completions endpoint
target.body:
## Bug Description

The `/v1/chat/completions` streaming endpoint emits SSE chunks that violate the OpenAI Chat Completions streaming spec:

- `finish_reason` is placed inside `delta` instead of at `choices[0]` level
- `finish_reason` is emitted on every chunk, not just the final one

## Root Cause

`writeAssistantContentChunk` placed `finish_reason` inside the `delta` object and always included it regardless of chunk position.

## Fix

- `writeAssistantContentChunk`: only attach `finish_reason` at `choices[0]` level when `isLast = true`
- Emit a final stop chunk (`delta: {}`, `finish_reason: "stop"`) when `phase === "end"` fires
- In error path: send error content as a regular chunk first, then send stop chunk separately
- Added `wroteFinalChunk` guard to prevent duplicate final chunk sends

## Test

Updated `openai-http.test.ts` streaming tests to verify:
- Middle chunks do NOT contain `finish_reason`
- Final chunk has `finish_reason: "stop"` at `choices[0]` level with empty `delta`
- Error path sends error content and stop chunk as separate chunks

## Impact

Any client consuming OpenClaw's streaming response with a strict OpenAI-compatible parser now works correctly.

---

Built with AI assistance (Claude Code / Codex).
