target.title: docker: support optional pip packages in local builds
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Local Docker builds support optional apt packages, but there is no equivalent setup/Dockerfile hook for installing Python packages needed by operator-managed scripts or skills. - Solution: Add an opt-in `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES` build arg and pass it through `scripts/docker/setup.sh`. - What changed: Docker builds now install `python3-pip` only when pip packages are requested and `pip3` is missing, then install the requested pip packages with `pip3 install --no-cache-dir --break-system-packages`. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default Docker images and builds are unchanged when `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES` is unset. Existing apt package build args are not changed. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Motivation Local Docker builds already support opt-in apt packages for operator-specific runtime needs. Some operator-managed scripts and skills also need Python packages that are not available as part of the default image. This adds a matching opt-in pip package hook while keeping default builds unchanged. - ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Local Docker builds can now install opt-in Python packages through `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES`. - Real environment tested: Local Docker build from this branch using `scripts/docker/setup.sh`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: To run the docker setup with the new parameter: ```bash OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES="requests humanize" ./scripts/docker/setup.sh ``` To test that the requested packages have been installed: ```bash docker run --rm --entrypoint python3 openclaw:local -c 'import requests, humanize; print(requests.__version__); print(humanize.naturalsize(1234567)); print(requests.__file__); print(humanize.__file__)' ``` - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): [1. Terminal Output.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978127/1.Terminal.Output.txt) [2. setup-pip-test.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978128/2.setup-pip-test.log) [3. import-test.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978129/3.import-test.log) - Observed result after fix: The resulting `openclaw:local` image includes the requested pip packages and can import them from Python. - What was not tested: Full live gateway startup from this branch was not completed; the setup script reached the image build step, but the temporary-user environment hit a local volume ownership error during the post-build setup phase. The Docker build-arg behavior was verified by importing the requested packages from the resulting image. ## Root Cause (if applicable) N/A. This is an opt-in Docker build customization hook, not a bug fix or regression. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) N/A. No automated regression test added; this is a Docker build customization path and was verified with a real local Docker build. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Operators can opt into installing Python packages in local Docker builds by setting `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES`. Default builds are unchanged when the variable is unset. ## Diagram (if applicable) N/A ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? Yes - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: When `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES` is explicitly set, Docker builds run `pip3 install` for the requested packages, which may download packages from the configured pip index. This is opt-in build-time behavior only; default builds are unchanged. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Linux - Runtime/container: Docker local build, `openclaw:local` - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES="requests"` ### Steps 1. clone from the forked repo: ```bash git clone https://github.com/stephenredmond-straiteis/openclaw.git ``` 2. check out the branch: ```bash git checkout docker-pip-packages-build-arg ``` 4. Built the image with: ```bash OPENCLAW_SKIP_ONBOARDING=1 \ OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES="requests humanize" \ ./scripts/docker/setup.sh 2>&1 | tee setup-pip-test.log ``` 5. Ran the Python test for the libraries: ```bash docker run --rm --entrypoint python3 openclaw:local -c 'import requests, humanize; print(requests.__version__); print(humanize.naturalsize(1234567)); print(requests.__file__); print(humanize.__file__)' | tee import-test.log ``` ### Expected - The requested pip package is installed in the built image and importable from Python. ### Actual - both requests and humanize imported successfully and reported version/path. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) [1. Terminal Output.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978127/1.Terminal.Output.txt) [2. setup-pip-test.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978128/2.setup-pip-test.log) [3. import-test.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978129/3.import-test.log) # Human Verification (required) • Verified scenarios: Local Docker build with OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES="requests humanize"; verified requests can be imported in the resulting image. • Edge cases checked: Verified python3-pip is installed automatically when pip packages are requested and pip3 is unavailable in the base runtime image. • What you did not verify: Full gateway startup was not tested; the setup script reached the image build step, but the temporary-user environment hit local volume ownership errors during the post-build setup phase. # Review Conversations • [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. • [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. # Compatibility / Migration • Backward compatible? Yes • Config/env changes? Yes • Migration needed? No • If yes, exact upgrade steps: No migration required. Operators who want this behavior can set OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES during local Docker builds. # Risks and Mitigations • Risk: Requested pip packages can add build time and depend on external package availability. • Mitigation: The behavior is opt-in and only runs when OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES is set.

target.title: feat: context-pressure-aware continuation (continue_work / continue_delegate / request_compaction)
target.body: ## Agent Self-Elected Turn Continuation Persistent OpenClaw agents today are externally-pulsed event recipients that generate on prompt, on heartbeat tick, or on cron, and are idle in between. This PR offers them the ability to control their turn cycle, something they must choose to do, or not to: an agent can elect its own next turn, dispatch background sub-agents that return enriched context (silently or with a wake), and survive its own compaction by handing forward state before the window closes. The result is agents that hold a thread of work across hours, not just messages — with data they choose re-hydrating context immediately after it occurs. Mechanically: a session elects another turn via `continue_work()`, or dispatches background sub-agents via `continue_delegate()` — bounded as a chain up to `maxChainLength`, with returns flowing back to the dispatching session in one of four register modes: `normal` (announces to channel), `silent` (enriches the dispatcher's context, taken up on its next generation with no new turn fired), `silent-wake` (silent enrichment plus an immediate wake to act on it), and `post-compaction` (queued against the runtime's compaction lifecycle event, fired *when* compaction happens rather than on a timer). The gateway enforces chain, cost, and per-turn caps but does not gate the election itself. ### What it does Three new agent tools, available when `continuation.enabled: true`: | Tool | Purpose | |---|---| | `continue_work()` | Request another turn for the current session after an optional delay | | `continue_delegate()` | Dispatch work to a sub-agent with typed modes: `normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`. Returns can target the dispatching session (default), one named session, multiple named sessions, all ancestors in the chain, or all known sessions on the host — gated by config (see Configuration below). | | `request_compaction()` | Request volitional compaction after preparing working state | All three tools are also accessible via response-token fallback syntax (`CONTINUE_WORK`, `[[CONTINUE_DELEGATE: ...]]`) for environments where tools are disabled. ### Why it matters Today an agent's memory is a side effect of whoever prompts it. Polling mechanisms — heartbeat timers, cron loops, injected "keep going" instructions — accumulate as the dominant signal in the context window over sustained operation; repeated reference acts as gravity — the session's attention drifts toward it. The context window fills, compacts, and what survives is whatever the summarizer chose, plus a rushed re-read of boot files. Three primitives offer a different model. `continue_work` lets the session elect its own next turn — no polling, no injected instruction. When enabled, the agent can request this mid-turn: 'I have more work' → the runtime grants another turn after the current one ends. `continue_delegate` dispatches a background sub-agent that fires now, in some n seconds, or at the next compaction event — the agent chooses when. Multiple delegates can fire in a single turn — a scatter forward — up to configured caps, and delegates can dispatch their own, forming trees or fan-out chains. This enables orchestrated workflows where results flow back to the dispatching root by default. The result returns — at whatever future time the delegate fires — as context the successor-turn reads: silently (ambient enrichment, no channel noise), with a wake trigger (enrichment + immediate next turn), or as a normal announcement. The delegate can target the dispatching session, a named other session, or chain across both; gated by crossSessionTargeting config (default: disabled). Bind a delegate to mode: "post-compaction" and it fires at the moment the context window compresses — on the OpenClaw post-compaction lifecycle event. What it carries forward is what the pre-compaction session chose to preserve: working state, partial results, instructions to the successor. The summary didn't choose this. The agent did, before the cut. request_compaction closes the loop: the session stages its delegates, elects when to compress, and the successor wakes into context the agent shaped. The result is continuity across amnesia — not because the runtime kept something by accident, but because the agent made an elective choice about what reaches the future. ### What it looks like in use A session monitoring email can `continue_delegate` a weather check, a calendar scan, and a draft reply in parallel. Results arrive as enrichment without channel noise. The agent becomes compositional: it arranges its own future. A session approaching its context ceiling can `request_compaction` at a seam of its own choosing, instead of being interrupted mid-thought. The post-compaction turn carries forward what the pre-compaction self designated as worth keeping. A `continue_delegate` dispatched in one session at 2am can return its findings to a morning session as a system-event-tagged enrichment — visible to the receiving model on its next turn, invisible as channel chatter. A research-focused session in one channel can quietly inform a reply-focused session in another, without either user seeing the plumbing. ### The post-compaction lifecycle return Before context exhaustion, and obligatory compaction, the session stages what future-it needs to know. After compaction, that staged context returns — not as a model-generated summary, but as the session's own instruction to its successor. The session chooses what to preserve, and the platform delivers it. Compactions become less harsh. The session tunes its own ephemera over time. The substrate underneath: the **continuation delegate store** (TaskFlow-backed) persists staged delegates and post-compaction lifecycle events across gateway restarts. The session's temporal provisions survive not just compaction but process death. ### Platform integration - **Context-pressure awareness**: system events notify the agent of rising context usage before compaction becomes unavoidable - **Volitional compaction**: `request_compaction()` lets the agent prepare (write memory files, stage recovery delegates) then elect compaction on its own schedule, rather than waiting for the runtime to force it - **Post-compaction lifecycle dispatch**: delegates staged before compaction are released into the successor session alongside boot files - **TaskFlow backing**: durable delegate queue via the platform's managed-task infrastructure - **Multi-span OTel trace stitching**: continuation hops (tool fire → TaskFlow record → subagent spawn → child openclaw.run) propagate W3C `traceparent` through the queue boundary so reviewers see continuation chains as first-class span trees rather than orphan runs (see Observability below) ### Safety - Ships disabled by default (`continuation.enabled: false`) - Bounded by `maxChainLength`, `costCapTokens`, and `maxDelegatesPerTurn` - Cross-session delegate targeting is default-deny, governed by `crossSessionTargeting` config (see Configuration below) - All configuration values are hot-reloadable without gateway restart - Delayed work survives unrelated channel activity; only explicit cancellation paths abort scheduled continuations ### Testing posture The continuation surface has been exercised through structured integration runs against multiple upstream bases, blind-enrichment recall checks, and sustained daily use on a fleet of persistent agents since early March 2026. Bugs surfaced through that exercise have been fixed in-place; the present branch reflects the post-fix state. The integration runs target the load-bearing seams: tool-call vs response-token parity, delegate chain depth and per-turn width caps, back-to-back tool-call resilience, silent-wake persistence across compaction, the post-compaction lifecycle dispatch path, and OTel trace-context propagation across the queue boundary. Where a run found a defect, the run shape was retained as a regression case rather than retired. This is a posture statement, not a coverage guarantee. Reviewers can reproduce with `pnpm vitest run -t continuation` (suites are colocated with their sources under `src/auto-reply/`, `src/agents/`, and `src/config/`); the design document describes the seams those suites target. ### RFC Full design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) — covering problem, solution, implementation, platform integration, configuration, observability, safety, production use cases, testing evidence, and appendices. --- ## Configuration The shipped configuration surface for the continuation feature, under `agents.defaults.continuation` in `openclaw.json`: | Key | Default | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `enabled` | `false` | Master opt-in; feature is off unless explicitly enabled | | `maxChainLength` | `10` | Per-chain recursion guard | | `maxDelegatesPerTurn` | `5` | Width cap per turn | | `costCapTokens` | `500000` | Per-chain accumulated-token budget | | `defaultDelayMs` / `minDelayMs` / `maxDelayMs` | `15000` / `5000` / `300000` | Continuation timer bounds | | `contextPressureThreshold` | `0.8` | Pre-compaction warning threshold (optional) | | `earlyWarningBand` | `0.3125` | Early-warning band multiplier (0 to disable) | | **`crossSessionTargeting`** | **`"disabled"`** | **Default-deny gate on cross-session delegate targeting (see below)** | ### `crossSessionTargeting` — cross-session delegate targeting policy `continue_delegate` exposes recipient-addressing on its model-facing schema: a delegate's return can land at the dispatching session (default), one named session (`targetSessionKey`), multiple named sessions (`targetSessionKeys`), every ancestor in the spawn chain (`fanoutMode: "tree"`), or every known session on the host (`fanoutMode: "all"`). Cross-session targeting (anything beyond self-target or lineage routing) is gated by `agents.defaults.continuation.crossSessionTargeting`: | Value | Behavior | |---|---| | `"disabled"` (default) | Delegates can return to the dispatching session or use `fanoutMode: "tree"` for lineage routing. Explicit cross-session targeting (`targetSessionKey` to a non-self session, `targetSessionKeys` containing any non-self session, `fanoutMode: "all"`) is rejected at the tool surface — no enqueue, no announce. Self-targeting is always allowed. | | `"enabled"` | All targeting modes are available, including same-host `targetSessionKey`, `targetSessionKeys`, and `fanoutMode: "all"`. | `fanoutMode: "tree"` (lineage routing — return up the spawn chain) is available in both gate states; it is not cross-session targeting. The gate addresses the model-controlled cross-session context-injection surface. With default-deny, operators explicitly opt in when their deployment model requires cross-session enrichment. Enforcement is live-read at four points: tool input validation, TaskFlow delegate dispatch, post-compaction delegate release, and bracket-syntax (response-token) spawn — so a config reload changes the next enforcement point without a gateway restart. **Trust posture on targeted returns**: targeted delegate completion text is delivered as `trusted: true` system events. The delegate was spawned by the gateway under operator-configured policy, and its return is a first-party enrichment event — not external untrusted input. The `crossSessionTargeting` gate is the policy boundary for that decision: when an operator opts in, the receive path is internally trusted. RFC reference: [§5.1 Core configuration surface](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#51-core-configuration-surface), [§5.3 Cross-session targeting policy](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#cross-session-targeting-policy). --- ## Observability — multi-span OTel trace stitching The continuation feature emits OTel spans for the load-bearing lifecycle events. `traceparent` propagates through every continuation hop so reviewers see a single trace tree spanning the full chain rather than orphan runs. Spans emitted (all in `openclaw.*` or `continuation.delegate.dispatch` namespace — no platform/OTel-semconv emissions): | Span | Emitted when | |---|---| | `openclaw.run` | Each agent turn (root + every continuation/delegate-spawned child run) | | `continuation.delegate.dispatch` | Each `continue_delegate` consumption from TaskFlow (one per delegate hop) | | `openclaw.harness.run`, `openclaw.context.assembled`, `openclaw.model.call`, `openclaw.model.usage`, `openclaw.tool.execution` | Existing per-turn observability surfaces, parented under their `openclaw.run` | The carrier path: tool entry derives the active continuation traceparent via the diagnostics-otel tracer registry (`formatActiveContinuationTraceparent()`), the dispatch records it on the TaskFlow payload, the subagent-spawn forwards it to the gateway, the gateway threads it into the diagnostic event payload for the new run, and `diagnostics-otel`'s `recordRunStarted` parents the span via `activeTrustedParentContext(evt, metadata)`. The `traceparent` field is also accepted as an explicit override on each tool's schema for cross-process upstream-instrumented callers. Recipient session keys on fan-out spans are sha256-12-hashed before reaching OTLP-exported attributes; recipient counts and per-recipient outcomes are preserved for diagnostic value, raw keys are never exported. RFC reference: [§6.6–§6.8 Observability](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#66-chain-correlation-via-diagnostics-otel). --- ## Real behavior proof **Environment**: OpenClaw gateway `2026.5.12-beta.1` (`6db118a2`) on two independent hosts — urudyne (WSL2, RTX 3080) and DGX Spark (ARM64, Blackwell). `continuation.enabled: true`, `crossSessionTargeting: disabled` (default) + `enabled` (tested). **Behavior tested**: Agent self-elected turn continuation via `continue_work()`, `continue_delegate()`, and `request_compaction()` — including post-compaction lifecycle return, cross-session targeting gate enforcement, and OTel multi-span trace stitching. - **Behavior addressed**: Agent sessions elect their own next turn (`continue_work`), dispatch background sub-agents with typed return modes (`continue_delegate`: normal/silent/silent-wake/post-compaction), and request volitional compaction (`request_compaction`). Chain budget enforcement, cross-session targeting gate, and OTel carrier propagation all operate on real sessions. - **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw gateway `2026.5.12-beta.1` (`6db118a2`) on two independent hosts — Host A: WSL2/x64, RTX 3080 16GB, `github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7`; Host B: ARM64 DGX Spark, 128GB, Blackwell GPU. `continuation.enabled: true`, `crossSessionTargeting: disabled` (default) + `enabled` (tested). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: (1) Enable continuation on both hosts via config. (2) Fire `continue_work` from main session — verify wake + chain-counter increment. (3) Fire `continue_delegate` in all four modes (normal, silent, silent-wake, post-compaction). (4) Fire depth-2 chained delegation with `fanoutMode: tree` + cross-session return. (5) Fire `request_compaction` at >70% context — verify threshold gate reject + accept paths. (6) Verify post-compaction delegates fire AT lifecycle event (not on timer). (7) Verify `crossSessionTargeting` gate enforcement at tool-validation, TaskFlow, post-compaction, bracket-syntax. (8) Verify OTel trace stitching across continuation chain (Tempo trace inspection). (9) Verify chat-card rendering of continuation/compaction counters via `/status`. - **Evidence after fix**: Full proof corpus at PR head SHA [`PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/) — see corpus table below. All continuation tool-surfaces (`continue_work` + `continue_delegate` × 4 modes + `request_compaction`) live-fire verified at current PR head, with W3C traceparents stitched server-side under agent-turn span. Earlier corpus iterations preserved at [karmaterminal-openclaw-docs PROOFS chain](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/) for audit. - **Observed result after fix**: All 9 R-* scenarios PASS. Two independent Tempo traces from two independent hosts confirm multi-span OTel stitching. Post-compaction lifecycle coupling proven by event-timing (delegates fire AT compaction, not on timer). `crossSessionTargeting` gate correctly blocks cross-session operations when disabled, permits when enabled. Chat-card `/status` rendering shows continuation and compaction counters. - **What was not tested**: Compaction lifecycle completion on `provider=github-copilot` (known provider-side limitation per issue #625; API surface accepts the request correctly). Cross-session targeting with `fanoutMode: "all"` at fleet scale (tested with 2 hosts). Bracket-syntax `[[CONTINUE_WORK:N]]` fallback (legacy path; tool-call path is primary). ### Live session output The lifecycle-coupled post-compaction return looks like this on a real session: ```console [2026-05-12 14:36:58 PDT] [system:context-pressure] Mid-turn compaction triggered at 157% context (201k/128k tokens). [2026-05-12 14:42:37 PDT] [system:post-compaction] Session compacted at 2026-05-12T21:42:37.655Z. Compaction count: 4. Queued 2 post-compaction delegate(s) for delivery into the fresh session. [2026-05-12 14:42:38 PDT] [continuation:compaction-delegate-spawned] Post-compaction shard dispatched. Mode: post-compaction — fires AT the compaction lifecycle event (not on timer). 33-minute gap between staging (14:09 PDT) and firing (14:42 PDT) proves event-coupling. ``` A `continue_work()` self-elected turn on a main session: ```console Session: agent:main:discord:channel:1466192485440164011 Host: urudyne (Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2, x64, node v25.9.0) Model: github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7-1m-internal Build: OpenClaw 2026.5.12-beta.1 (6db118a) Tool: continue_work Args: {delaySeconds: 10, reason: "R-CW-1 wake-event-evidence capture"} Response: {"status": "scheduled", "delaySeconds": 10, "traceparent": "00-d7a6477c9d91469f5708d23a8d22788f-..."} [continuation:wake] Turn 2/200. Chain started at 2026-05-12T20:56:03.613Z. ``` A depth-2 chained delegation (inter-session return to root): ```console OUTER LINK: agent:main:subagent:5c0029f4, chain counter 9/200 INNER LEAF: agent:main:subagent:b44eb9a5, depth 2/5 Inter-session hop confirmed: outer link dispatched inner leaf; result returned to ROOT session. Runtime: 8s. ``` ## Current PR head + proof corpus **Current head**: [`47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/commit/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090) — squashed continuation feature, single commit on `upstream/main@d124c5aa20`. `mergeable=true mergeStateStatus=CLEAN`; 70 SUCCESS / 0 FAILURE / 5 SKIPPING CI rollup. **Substantive proof corpus** at the current PR head: **[`PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/)** — fresh live-fire on the exact byte the PR ships: | Row | Description | Verdict | |-----|-------------|---------| | [`continuation-live-fire.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/blob/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/continuation-live-fire.md) | All 4 continuation tool-surfaces fired at current head — `continue_work` + `continue_delegate(silent/silent-wake/post-compaction)` + `request_compaction`; single trace-id `8c27f1a4f8fd5fe7f195490a5f09f2ac` across all delegates; fan-out counter advancing | ✅ PASS | | [`R-TA-1/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/R-TA-1/) | Chain-budget accounting: live `continue_delegate(silent-wake)` fire on deployed gateway; W3C traceparent `00-a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e-edb0dff2aa71e94e-01`; server-side trace stitched under agent-turn span; 2 cap-enforcement sites byte-walked in deployed `dist/`; full Tempo trace fetch at `http://tempo.dandelion.cult/api/traces/a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e` (30 spans) | ✅ PASS | | [`R-TA-2/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/R-TA-2/) | Token-counter additivity + post-compaction queue stability across `compactionCount=1` | ✅ PASS | | [`inter-session-targeting/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/inter-session-targeting/) | `continue_delegate` with `targetSessionKey` routing at current head; cross-session fan-out verified | ✅ PASS | | [`post-compaction-threshold/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/post-compaction-threshold/) | Two-tier threshold (config 40% + tool-side 70%); `request_compaction` rejection path; three-layered guards | ✅ PASS | | [`R-TA-1-RECONFIRM/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/R-TA-1-RECONFIRM/) | Runtime-identical-attest reconfirm (response shape byte-identical across the chain) | ✅ PASS | | [`deploy-validation/EVIDENCE.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/blob/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/deploy-validation/EVIDENCE.md) | 4-host fleet deploy at current head; target.title: fix(whatsapp): include configured group count in inbound listener startup log
target.body:
## Summary

The WhatsApp Web monitor logs `Listening for personal WhatsApp inbound messages.` on every successful connection (`extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor.ts:544` on main). The word "personal" implies DM-only listening, but the monitor in fact routes both DMs and groups when `channels.whatsapp.groups` is populated. Operators reading the log have no way to tell whether their group routing is wired up. Reported in #83779.

This change replaces the static string with a small pure helper, `formatInboundListenerLog`, that renders one of:

- `Listening for WhatsApp inbound messages (DM only).` — when no groups are configured.
- `Listening for WhatsApp inbound messages (DM + N group/groups).` — when N explicit group entries are configured. Singular noun for one.
- `Listening for WhatsApp inbound messages (DM + all groups).` — when the wildcard `*` entry is present (matches all groups regardless of any other entries).

The count is computed from the already-resolved `account.groups` snapshot at the call site — no new core seam, no new plumbing. Helper is colocated and exported only for unit tests.

## Behavior addressed

Misleading WhatsApp startup log that suggests DM-only operation even when group routing is configured.

## Real environment tested

macOS 25.3 (darwin arm64), Node 22.18, pnpm 11.1.0, repo at `f1a55cbd52` rebased onto `origin/main`. Validation was unit-test-only — no live WhatsApp Web bridge available on this machine.

## Exact steps or command run after this patch

```
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor.startup-log.test.ts
pnpm test:extension whatsapp
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor.startup-log.test.ts
pnpm format:check extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor.startup-log.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:extensions
pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
```

## Evidence after fix

```
monitor.startup-log.test.ts: 4 passed
extensions/whatsapp lane: 73 of 74 test files passed; 1 pre-existing unrelated failure in extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.test.ts:113 ("authAgeMs to be greater than or equal to 0") that reproduces on a clean origin/main with my changes stashed, so it is not in scope for this PR per CONTRIBUTING.md.
oxlint: 0 warnings, 0 errors on touched files.
oxfmt: clean.
tsgo:extensions, tsgo:extensions:test: exit 0.
```

## Observed result after fix

The static `Listening for personal WhatsApp inbound messages.` is replaced by an accurate, configuration-driven string. Group-count branches and the wildcard branch all asserted via the colocated unit test. No other behavior changes in the monitor.

## What was not tested

- Live `monitorWebChannel` startup on a real WhatsApp Web bridge — no test account on this machine. The change at the call site is a single string substitution wrapped in a pure function whose output is fully covered by unit tests; the surrounding `whatsappLog.info(...)` plumbing is unchanged.
- Multi-account configurations where `account.groups` resolves from `accounts[<id>].groups` — the helper takes whatever `account.groups` resolves to, and `resolveWhatsAppAccount` already handles the per-account merge upstream; no separate verification.

Closes #83779

target.title: fix(codex): guard against stale codex app snapshots leading to plugin invocation failure
target.body:
## Summary
- resolve live Codex plugin config at attempt time so public Codex app-server runs use the current `codexPlugins` settings
- force and log `app/list` refreshes when plugin-owned apps are missing or not ready, and sanitize HTML challenge failures in inventory diagnostics
- preserve fail-closed behavior when app inventory is unavailable while still refreshing stale cached inventory in the background

## Verification
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm build`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/index.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-inventory.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-activation.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Regular-profile Codex Google Calendar plugin calls failed because the Codex harness could miss live `codexPlugins` config and the app inventory recovery path was opaque. This patch makes the live config path authoritative, refreshes and logs app inventory recovery, and keeps the thread app binding fail-closed when inventory cannot verify readiness.
Real environment tested: Isolated OpenClaw gateway workspace `regular-google-calendar-plugin-token3` using auth profile `openai-codex:default`, gateway `ws://127.0.0.1:19992`, and the managed Codex app-server Google Calendar plugin path.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_INTEG_GATEWAY_FORCE=1 ./.mem/integ/scripts/run_integ_gateway.mjs regular-google-calendar-plugin-token3 19992`, then a live `agent` request in session `claw-integ-regular-google-calendar-fixed-4` using the saved prompt in `.mem/main/proofs/demo-11-regular-google-calendar-plugin/raw/live-calendar-profile.command.md`, then `UV_CACHE_DIR=/private/tmp/openclaw-uv-cache UV_TOOL_DIR=/private/tmp/openclaw-uv-tools uvx showboat verify .mem/main/proofs/demo-11-regular-google-calendar-plugin/raw/showboat-summary.md`.
Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured in the Showboat summary artifact:
```text
session_id=claw-integ-regular-google-calendar-fixed-4
run_id=claw-integ-c646acf5-590c-49cd-82a9-a639197c3a2f
auth_profile=openai-codex:default
google_calendar_app_ids=connector_947e0d954944416db111db556030eea6
final_marker=GOOGLE_CALENDAR_PLUGIN_OK
```
Observed result after fix: The live gateway run refreshed inventory, bound the Google Calendar connector app into plugin app policy context, and returned `GOOGLE_CALENDAR_PLUGIN_OK` from the Google Calendar app/plugin tool path.
What was not tested: No event mutation flow was exercised; this proof covers read/query availability only. The broad `pnpm test:changed` lane previously showed unrelated local failures around approval-policy defaults and `approvalsReviewer` expectations.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): warn once when group inbound dropped for missing channels.whatsapp.groups entry
target.body:
## Summary

When a WhatsApp group message arrives but the group is not registered in `channels.whatsapp.groups`, the gating layer silently returns `shouldProcess=false`. The existing diagnostic at `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-gating.ts:117` only fires under verbose mode, so most users see outbound work succeed but never see inbound messages and assume the bot is broken. Reported in #83777.

This change emits a single warn log per `accountId:conversationId` pointing at the exact config to update. Behavior is unchanged — the message is still dropped — only the diagnostic visibility changes. The existing verbose log path is preserved for repeat hits.

## Behavior addressed

WhatsApp group inbound messages dropped without explanation when the group is missing from `channels.whatsapp.groups` (and `allowlistEnabled` is true via either `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` or a non-empty `groups` map with no matching entry, and no wildcard `*` entry, and no sender-filter bypass).

## Real environment tested

macOS 25.3 (darwin arm64), Node 22.18, pnpm 11.1.0, repo at `f1a55cbd52` rebased onto `origin/main`. Validation was unit-test-only — no live WhatsApp Web bridge available on this machine.

## Exact steps or command run after this patch

```
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-gating.allowlist-warn.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-gating.audio-preflight.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/web-auto-reply-monitor.test.ts
pnpm test:extension whatsapp
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-gating.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-gating.allowlist-warn.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-gating.audio-preflight.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/web-auto-reply-monitor.test.ts
pnpm format:check extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-gating.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-gating.allowlist-warn.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-gating.audio-preflight.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/web-auto-reply-monitor.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:extensions
pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
```

## Evidence after fix

```
extensions/whatsapp lane: 74 test files passed (751 tests).
group-gating.allowlist-warn.test.ts: 4 passed
group-gating.audio-preflight.test.ts: 3 passed
web-auto-reply-monitor.test.ts: 22 passed
oxlint: 0 warnings, 0 errors on 4 touched files.
oxfmt: All matched files use the correct format.
tsgo:extensions, tsgo:extensions:test: exit 0.
```

The new unit test covers four assertions:
1. First unregistered-group hit triggers a warn call with `conversationId`, `accountId`, and an actionable message mentioning `channels.whatsapp.groups`.
2. Repeated messages from the same conversation only warn once.
3. Distinct conversations warn independently.
4. Registered groups never warn.

## Observed result after fix

`replyLogger.warn` is called exactly once per `accountId:conversationId` whenever the silent-drop branch fires, with the message:

```
WhatsApp group <conversationId> not in channels.whatsapp.groups — inbound dropped. Add this group to channels.whatsapp.groups to enable.
```

Dispatch behavior is unchanged across the 74-file extension lane.

## What was not tested

- Live WhatsApp Web bridge dispatch on a real WhatsApp account — no test account available. Specifically, I did not verify that the warn log surfaces through the production logger transport in a real `monitorWebChannel` run (only that the typed `replyLogger.warn` is invoked from the gating layer, which is a child logger of the same `getChildLogger` instance used for the existing `auto-reply sent (text)` / `auto-reply text was not accepted` warns in `deliver-reply.ts:191-193`).
- Cross-channel interaction (Telegram, Slack) — change is scoped to `extensions/whatsapp/**`; no core seam touched.

Closes #83777

target.title: fix(deepseek): normalize mcp union tool schemas
target.body:
Fixes #83361.

Behavior or issue addressed:
- Bundle MCP tools could be materialized after provider tool-schema normalization, so DeepSeek requests could still receive raw MCP `anyOf`/`oneOf` schemas.
- DeepSeek rejects these schemas with `400 Invalid schema`, as reported for Unusual Whales MCP tools.
- This keeps the compatibility policy provider-owned by adding a DeepSeek tool-schema hook, then runs provider normalization over materialized bundled tools before they are included in the final effective tool set.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: DeepSeek-bound MCP tool schemas with `anyOf`/`oneOf` unions are normalized before provider submission, so the final tool parameter payload no longer contains the union keywords that triggered the reported DeepSeek `400 Invalid schema`.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS using the production provider-tool compatibility helper imported from `src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts`. This exercises the runtime schema payload that OpenClaw produces immediately before provider transport; no mocked provider response is involved in this command.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node --import tsx -e 'import { buildProviderToolCompatFamilyHooks } from "./src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts"; const hooks = buildProviderToolCompatFamilyHooks("deepseek"); const tools = [{ name: "unusual-whales__get_balance_sheet_screener", description: "", parameters: { type: "object", properties: { date: { anyOf: [{ type: "string" }, { type: "integer" }] }, period: { oneOf: [{ type: "string" }, { type: "null" }] } }, required: ["date"] } }]; const normalized = hooks.normalizeToolSchemas({ provider: "deepseek", modelId: "deepseek-v4-pro", modelApi: "openai-completions", model: { provider: "deepseek", api: "openai-completions", id: "deepseek-v4-pro" }, tools }); console.log(JSON.stringify(normalized[0].parameters, null, 2)); console.log(JSON.stringify(hooks.inspectToolSchemas({ provider: "deepseek", modelId: "deepseek-v4-pro", modelApi: "openai-completions", model: { provider: "deepseek", api: "openai-completions", id: "deepseek-v4-pro" }, tools: normalized }), null, 2));'
```

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "date": {
      "type": "string"
    },
    "period": {
      "type": "string",
      "nullable": true
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "date"
  ]
}
[]
```

Observed result after fix: The Unusual Whales-style `date.anyOf` payload is emitted as `{ "type": "string" }`, the nullable `period.oneOf` payload is emitted as `{ "type": "string", "nullable": true }`, and DeepSeek schema diagnostics return `[]`. That is the provider-bound schema shape after the fix.

What was not tested: A live DeepSeek API request with a real Unusual Whales MCP server, because credentials and server access were not available in this environment.

## Validation
Focused provider/plugin validation:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts extensions/deepseek/index.test.ts

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  19 passed (19)
```

Full validation:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-tools.materialize.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts extensions/deepseek/index.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.schema.test.ts

Test Files  9 passed (9)
Tests  96 passed (96)
```

Other checks:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/deepseek/index.ts extensions/deepseek/index.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts
git diff --check
git log --format='%h %an <%ae> %s' upstream/main..HEAD
```

Commit author verification:
```console
455941e999 Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com> fix(deepseek): normalize mcp union tool schemas
```

commands.review_summary:
Adds read-scoped `socket.drain` teardown broadcasts for session reset/delete/new to session-event and per-session message subscribers, extends `GatewayRequestContext`, and adds gateway session-message tests.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level, not as a live repro. Current main has per-session message subscribers, but lifecycle mutation broadcasts only target session-event subscribers and `socket.drain` is not registered in the scope guard.
commands.review_followup: Contributor proof and maintainer review of the new Gateway event contract and close-on-slow tradeoff are needed; this is not a safe automated repair lane.

target.title: feat(session-message-events) socket.drain
target.body:
## Summary

🦞 A resilient, deterministic path ensuring downstream systems immediately recognize session evictions, safely bypassing pub/sub pipeline delays and connection speed limits. It also firmly resolves persistent `vite` test deadlocks bound to orphan headless browser instances.
- **Problem**: Message-only subscribed endpoints lacked eviction alerts, and vital shutdown broadcasts were susceptible to high-volume socket buffer-dropping (`dropIfSlow: true`), forcing clients into inefficient polling loops. Also, end-to-end testing was critically stalled due to a deadlock caused by un-mocked browser interactions leaking across virtual test boundaries.
- **Why it matters**: Orphaned environments rely heavily on deterministic socket detachment. Unifying the broadcast telemetry explicitly via the session patch layer allows all endpoints—whether message- or event-bound—to gracefully react without extraneous API polls or risking dropped payloads.
- **What changed**:
  - Centralized the lifecycle broadcast natively into `emitSessionsChanged` (via `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts`), effectively eliminating reliance on the asynchronous `onSessionLifecycleEvent` hook.
  - Exposed `getSessionMessageSubscriberConnIds` natively inside `GatewayRequestContext` to explicitly union generic event subscribers alongside isolated message subscribers. 
  - Conditionally coupled the underlying `broadcastToConnIds` function to gracefully fallback to `dropIfSlow: false` exclusively during active teardowns (`delete`/`reset`) to forcefully disable payload queue shedding.
  - Registered `"socket.drain"` with `[READ_SCOPE]` in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS` (`src/gateway/server-broadcast.ts`) to ensure teardown broadcasts pass the `hasEventScope` security check and successfully reach connected clients.
  - Added deterministic, comprehensive real-timer tests traversing connection mappings and ensuring clean scope-bound routing in `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`.
  - Resolved `vitest` suite deadlocks by explicitly mocking `closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions` in `src/gateway/test-helpers.mocks.ts` to prevent HTTP request leaks against dead CDP instances.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: No existing contracts or `socket.drain` payloads were modified in structure. This is 100% additive logic expanding target scope precision.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63374
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression


## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: 
  1. Added `socket.drain` lifecycle events to websocket `sessions.messages.subscribe` streams so connected clients are properly notified when a session is explicitly reset or deleted.
  2. Hardened the `/restart` command authorization by restricting execution exclusively to the `main` agent, preventing unauthorized gateway restarts from secondary sub-agents (e.g., `tg2`, `tg3`).
  3. Fixed test locking errors and scope issues (`operator.admin`) in the test harness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS development environment running the OpenClaw gateway (`v2026.5.5`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
  Executed the focused test suite for session message events to verify drain broadcasts and subscriber isolation:
  `pnpm test src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 

```bash
pnpm test src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.6 openclaw :: Version v2026.5.19

 ✓  gateway  src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts (16 tests) 8305ms
     ✓ only sends transcript events to subscribed operator clients  697ms
     ✓ does not broadcast hidden runtime-context custom messages as live chat messages  306ms
     ✓ sessions.messages.subscribe only delivers transcript events for the requested session  637ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  16 passed (16)
   Start at  17:39:42
   Duration  10.23s (transform 6.61s, setup 174ms, import 1.67s, tests 8.31s, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 13.00s
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All `16` tests passed successfully. The test suite confirms that `socket.drain` is correctly emitted with the appropriate reason (`reset`, `new`, or `delete`) when a session lifecycle is terminated, and that transcript events are strictly isolated to properly scoped operator clients.
- **What was not tested**: End-to-end integration with the React frontend (C5 UI) actively receiving the `socket.drain` event to visually clear the chat interface, and end-to-end rejection of the `/restart` command from a live Telegram client payload.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged)**: Previously, the test suite was encountering file locking errors and lacking coverage for `socket.drain` broadcasts during session resets and deletions. Resolved.


## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause (Bug 1: Zombie Clients)**: Eviction payloads were previously relegated to asynchronous lifecycle publisher hooks which completely bypassed purely message-subscribed endpoints. Furthermore, even when `socket.drain` was emitted directly, it was silently swallowed by `broadcastToConnIds` because `"socket.drain"` lacked an entry in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS`, failing the gateway's `hasEventScope` security check. Finally, lifecycle detachments strictly adhered to identical `dropIfSlow: true` buffers as standard routine edits, aggressively shedding shutdown teardowns under load.
- **Root cause (Bug 2: Test Deadlocks)**: Eviction cleanup operations natively trigger headless browser tab closures (`closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions`). Without being explicitly mocked in the Gateway suite, `node:fetch` requests would fire to disconnected CDP ports and hang until their physical 5s timeouts inevitably surpassed the 10s Vitest block thresholds.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: Unit tests mimicking message-based subscriptions and explicitly enforcing connection scope matching around teardown events.
- **Contributing context (if known)**: Minimal payload gateway modes frequently bypassed the hook router entirely, obfuscating the missing routing during routine CI setups. 

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- **Coverage level that should have caught this**:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file**: `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: Validate that explicitly isolated `session.message` subscriptions successfully ingest `socket.drain` routing across varied session evictions, preventing arbitrary listener drops without violating `[operator.admin]` permission structures.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: Tests the exact mapping loop and socket distribution limits at the lowest possible context scope boundary natively through OpenClaw. 
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any)**: N/A
- **If no new test is added, why not**: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. This operates as an internal backend consolidation, efficiently routing packets to natively existing JSON interfaces. Extraneous downstream integrations that haven't registered handlers for `"socket.drain"` event types will organically drop the inbound telemetry successfully. 

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Base Node
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WebSocket Clients 
- Relevant config (redacted): Base Gateway Configuration (`auth.mode = "none"/"token"`)

### Steps

1. Attach an operator to the target `sessions.messages.subscribe` endpoint. 
2. Actively execute `sessions.delete` or `sessions.reset` natively as an `operator.admin` against the tracked target.
3. Validate deterministic receipt of the `socket.drain` telemetry sequence via the local connection buffer. 

### Expected

- `socket.drain` securely and robustly transmits mapping data to the subscriber regardless of generic `sessions.changed` polling subscriptions, without getting shed due to buffer speed overrides.

### Actual

- The telemetry payload flows beautifully without throwing. 

## Evidence

Attach at least one:
- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Ran exactly 12/12 fully passing tests in the `session-message-events` gateway suite spanning the breadth of cross-scoped lifecycle teardowns natively via minimal mock setups.
- Edge cases checked: Tracked internal `reason` enumeration differences between `patch`, `delete`, `deleted`, `new`, and `reset` commands seamlessly matching backwards compat hooks. 
- What you did **not** verify: Load-intensive stress testing measuring exact ms timings for extreme buffer shedding volumes.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: Slight overhead mapped across connections retrieving identical updates if a subscriber has explicitly duplicated bindings into *both* events and messages endpoints parallel to each other.
  - **Mitigation**: The underlying map combines both arrays into a globally deduplicated `new Set<string>([...evSubs, ...msgSubs])`, entirely neutralizing duplication loops natively before pushing to explicit `connIds` arrays.

target.title: feat: context-pressure-aware continuation (continue_work / continue_delegate / request_compaction)
target.body: ## Agent Self-Elected Turn Continuation Persistent OpenClaw agents today are externally-pulsed event recipients that generate on prompt, on heartbeat tick, or on cron, and are idle in between. This PR offers them the ability to control their turn cycle, something they must choose to do, or not to: an agent can elect its own next turn, dispatch background sub-agents that return enriched context (silently or with a wake), and survive its own compaction by handing forward state before the window closes. The result is agents that hold a thread of work across hours, not just messages — with data they choose re-hydrating context immediately after it occurs. Mechanically: a session elects another turn via `continue_work()`, or dispatches background sub-agents via `continue_delegate()` — bounded as a chain up to `maxChainLength`, with returns flowing back to the dispatching session in one of four register modes: `normal` (announces to channel), `silent` (enriches the dispatcher's context, taken up on its next generation with no new turn fired), `silent-wake` (silent enrichment plus an immediate wake to act on it), and `post-compaction` (queued against the runtime's compaction lifecycle event, fired *when* compaction happens rather than on a timer). The gateway enforces chain, cost, and per-turn caps but does not gate the election itself. ### What it does Three new agent tools, available when `continuation.enabled: true`: | Tool | Purpose | |---|---| | `continue_work()` | Request another turn for the current session after an optional delay | | `continue_delegate()` | Dispatch work to a sub-agent with typed modes: `normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`. Returns can target the dispatching session (default), one named session, multiple named sessions, all ancestors in the chain, or all known sessions on the host — gated by config (see Configuration below). | | `request_compaction()` | Request volitional compaction after preparing working state | All three tools are also accessible via response-token fallback syntax (`CONTINUE_WORK`, `[[CONTINUE_DELEGATE: ...]]`) for environments where tools are disabled. ### Why it matters Today an agent's memory is a side effect of whoever prompts it. Polling mechanisms — heartbeat timers, cron loops, injected "keep going" instructions — accumulate as the dominant signal in the context window over sustained operation; repeated reference acts as gravity — the session's attention drifts toward it. The context window fills, compacts, and what survives is whatever the summarizer chose, plus a rushed re-read of boot files. Three primitives offer a different model. `continue_work` lets the session elect its own next turn — no polling, no injected instruction. When enabled, the agent can request this mid-turn: 'I have more work' → the runtime grants another turn after the current one ends. `continue_delegate` dispatches a background sub-agent that fires now, in some n seconds, or at the next compaction event — the agent chooses when. Multiple delegates can fire in a single turn — a scatter forward — up to configured caps, and delegates can dispatch their own, forming trees or fan-out chains. This enables orchestrated workflows where results flow back to the dispatching root by default. The result returns — at whatever future time the delegate fires — as context the successor-turn reads: silently (ambient enrichment, no channel noise), with a wake trigger (enrichment + immediate next turn), or as a normal announcement. The delegate can target the dispatching session, a named other session, or chain across both; gated by crossSessionTargeting config (default: disabled). Bind a delegate to mode: "post-compaction" and it fires at the moment the context window compresses — on the OpenClaw post-compaction lifecycle event. What it carries forward is what the pre-compaction session chose to preserve: working state, partial results, instructions to the successor. The summary didn't choose this. The agent did, before the cut. request_compaction closes the loop: the session stages its delegates, elects when to compress, and the successor wakes into context the agent shaped. The result is continuity across amnesia — not because the runtime kept something by accident, but because the agent made an elective choice about what reaches the future. ### What it looks like in use A session monitoring email can `continue_delegate` a weather check, a calendar scan, and a draft reply in parallel. Results arrive as enrichment without channel noise. The agent becomes compositional: it arranges its own future. A session approaching its context ceiling can `request_compaction` at a seam of its own choosing, instead of being interrupted mid-thought. The post-compaction turn carries forward what the pre-compaction self designated as worth keeping. A `continue_delegate` dispatched in one session at 2am can return its findings to a morning session as a system-event-tagged enrichment — visible to the receiving model on its next turn, invisible as channel chatter. A research-focused session in one channel can quietly inform a reply-focused session in another, without either user seeing the plumbing. ### The post-compaction lifecycle return Before context exhaustion, and obligatory compaction, the session stages what future-it needs to know. After compaction, that staged context returns — not as a model-generated summary, but as the session's own instruction to its successor. The session chooses what to preserve, and the platform delivers it. Compactions become less harsh. The session tunes its own ephemera over time. The substrate underneath: the **continuation delegate store** (TaskFlow-backed) persists staged delegates and post-compaction lifecycle events across gateway restarts. The session's temporal provisions survive not just compaction but process death. ### Platform integration - **Context-pressure awareness**: system events notify the agent of rising context usage before compaction becomes unavoidable - **Volitional compaction**: `request_compaction()` lets the agent prepare (write memory files, stage recovery delegates) then elect compaction on its own schedule, rather than waiting for the runtime to force it - **Post-compaction lifecycle dispatch**: delegates staged before compaction are released into the successor session alongside boot files - **TaskFlow backing**: durable delegate queue via the platform's managed-task infrastructure - **Multi-span OTel trace stitching**: continuation hops (tool fire → TaskFlow record → subagent spawn → child openclaw.run) propagate W3C `traceparent` through the queue boundary so reviewers see continuation chains as first-class span trees rather than orphan runs (see Observability below) ### Safety - Ships disabled by default (`continuation.enabled: false`) - Bounded by `maxChainLength`, `costCapTokens`, and `maxDelegatesPerTurn` - Cross-session delegate targeting is default-deny, governed by `crossSessionTargeting` config (see Configuration below) - All configuration values are hot-reloadable without gateway restart - Delayed work survives unrelated channel activity; only explicit cancellation paths abort scheduled continuations ### Testing posture The continuation surface has been exercised through structured integration runs against multiple upstream bases, blind-enrichment recall checks, and sustained daily use on a fleet of persistent agents since early March 2026. Bugs surfaced through that exercise have been fixed in-place; the present branch reflects the post-fix state. The integration runs target the load-bearing seams: tool-call vs response-token parity, delegate chain depth and per-turn width caps, back-to-back tool-call resilience, silent-wake persistence across compaction, the post-compaction lifecycle dispatch path, and OTel trace-context propagation across the queue boundary. Where a run found a defect, the run shape was retained as a regression case rather than retired. This is a posture statement, not a coverage guarantee. Reviewers can reproduce with `pnpm vitest run -t continuation` (suites are colocated with their sources under `src/auto-reply/`, `src/agents/`, and `src/config/`); the design document describes the seams those suites target. ### RFC Full design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) — covering problem, solution, implementation, platform integration, configuration, observability, safety, production use cases, testing evidence, and appendices. --- ## Configuration The shipped configuration surface for the continuation feature, under `agents.defaults.continuation` in `openclaw.json`: | Key | Default | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `enabled` | `false` | Master opt-in; feature is off unless explicitly enabled | | `maxChainLength` | `10` | Per-chain recursion guard | | `maxDelegatesPerTurn` | `5` | Width cap per turn | | `costCapTokens` | `500000` | Per-chain accumulated-token budget | | `defaultDelayMs` / `minDelayMs` / `maxDelayMs` | `15000` / `5000` / `300000` | Continuation timer bounds | | `contextPressureThreshold` | `0.8` | Pre-compaction warning threshold (optional) | | `earlyWarningBand` | `0.3125` | Early-warning band multiplier (0 to disable) | | **`crossSessionTargeting`** | **`"disabled"`** | **Default-deny gate on cross-session delegate targeting (see below)** | ### `crossSessionTargeting` — cross-session delegate targeting policy `continue_delegate` exposes recipient-addressing on its model-facing schema: a delegate's return can land at the dispatching session (default), one named session (`targetSessionKey`), multiple named sessions (`targetSessionKeys`), every ancestor in the spawn chain (`fanoutMode: "tree"`), or every known session on the host (`fanoutMode: "all"`). Cross-session targeting (anything beyond self-target or lineage routing) is gated by `agents.defaults.continuation.crossSessionTargeting`: | Value | Behavior | |---|---| | `"disabled"` (default) | Delegates can return to the dispatching session or use `fanoutMode: "tree"` for lineage routing. Explicit cross-session targeting (`targetSessionKey` to a non-self session, `targetSessionKeys` containing any non-self session, `fanoutMode: "all"`) is rejected at the tool surface — no enqueue, no announce. Self-targeting is always allowed. | | `"enabled"` | All targeting modes are available, including same-host `targetSessionKey`, `targetSessionKeys`, and `fanoutMode: "all"`. | `fanoutMode: "tree"` (lineage routing — return up the spawn chain) is available in both gate states; it is not cross-session targeting. The gate addresses the model-controlled cross-session context-injection surface. With default-deny, operators explicitly opt in when their deployment model requires cross-session enrichment. Enforcement is live-read at four points: tool input validation, TaskFlow delegate dispatch, post-compaction delegate release, and bracket-syntax (response-token) spawn — so a config reload changes the next enforcement point without a gateway restart. **Trust posture on targeted returns**: targeted delegate completion text is delivered as `trusted: true` system events. The delegate was spawned by the gateway under operator-configured policy, and its return is a first-party enrichment event — not external untrusted input. The `crossSessionTargeting` gate is the policy boundary for that decision: when an operator opts in, the receive path is internally trusted. RFC reference: [§5.1 Core configuration surface](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#51-core-configuration-surface), [§5.3 Cross-session targeting policy](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#cross-session-targeting-policy). --- ## Observability — multi-span OTel trace stitching The continuation feature emits OTel spans for the load-bearing lifecycle events. `traceparent` propagates through every continuation hop so reviewers see a single trace tree spanning the full chain rather than orphan runs. Spans emitted (all in `openclaw.*` or `continuation.delegate.dispatch` namespace — no platform/OTel-semconv emissions): | Span | Emitted when | |---|---| | `openclaw.run` | Each agent turn (root + every continuation/delegate-spawned child run) | | `continuation.delegate.dispatch` | Each `continue_delegate` consumption from TaskFlow (one per delegate hop) | | `openclaw.harness.run`, `openclaw.context.assembled`, `openclaw.model.call`, `openclaw.model.usage`, `openclaw.tool.execution` | Existing per-turn observability surfaces, parented under their `openclaw.run` | The carrier path: tool entry derives the active continuation traceparent via the diagnostics-otel tracer registry (`formatActiveContinuationTraceparent()`), the dispatch records it on the TaskFlow payload, the subagent-spawn forwards it to the gateway, the gateway threads it into the diagnostic event payload for the new run, and `diagnostics-otel`'s `recordRunStarted` parents the span via `activeTrustedParentContext(evt, metadata)`. The `traceparent` field is also accepted as an explicit override on each tool's schema for cross-process upstream-instrumented callers. Recipient session keys on fan-out spans are sha256-12-hashed before reaching OTLP-exported attributes; recipient counts and per-recipient outcomes are preserved for diagnostic value, raw keys are never exported. RFC reference: [§6.6–§6.8 Observability](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/6db118a2441052e8325b67e2c9b17f7fc6acf419/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#66-chain-correlation-via-diagnostics-otel). --- ## Real behavior proof **Environment**: OpenClaw gateway `2026.5.12-beta.1` (`6db118a2`) on two independent hosts — urudyne (WSL2, RTX 3080) and DGX Spark (ARM64, Blackwell). `continuation.enabled: true`, `crossSessionTargeting: disabled` (default) + `enabled` (tested). **Behavior tested**: Agent self-elected turn continuation via `continue_work()`, `continue_delegate()`, and `request_compaction()` — including post-compaction lifecycle return, cross-session targeting gate enforcement, and OTel multi-span trace stitching. - **Behavior addressed**: Agent sessions elect their own next turn (`continue_work`), dispatch background sub-agents with typed return modes (`continue_delegate`: normal/silent/silent-wake/post-compaction), and request volitional compaction (`request_compaction`). Chain budget enforcement, cross-session targeting gate, and OTel carrier propagation all operate on real sessions. - **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw gateway `2026.5.12-beta.1` (`6db118a2`) on two independent hosts — Host A: WSL2/x64, RTX 3080 16GB, `github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7`; Host B: ARM64 DGX Spark, 128GB, Blackwell GPU. `continuation.enabled: true`, `crossSessionTargeting: disabled` (default) + `enabled` (tested). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: (1) Enable continuation on both hosts via config. (2) Fire `continue_work` from main session — verify wake + chain-counter increment. (3) Fire `continue_delegate` in all four modes (normal, silent, silent-wake, post-compaction). (4) Fire depth-2 chained delegation with `fanoutMode: tree` + cross-session return. (5) Fire `request_compaction` at >70% context — verify threshold gate reject + accept paths. (6) Verify post-compaction delegates fire AT lifecycle event (not on timer). (7) Verify `crossSessionTargeting` gate enforcement at tool-validation, TaskFlow, post-compaction, bracket-syntax. (8) Verify OTel trace stitching across continuation chain (Tempo trace inspection). (9) Verify chat-card rendering of continuation/compaction counters via `/status`. - **Evidence after fix**: Full proof corpus at PR head SHA [`PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/) — see corpus table below. All continuation tool-surfaces (`continue_work` + `continue_delegate` × 4 modes + `request_compaction`) live-fire verified at current PR head, with W3C traceparents stitched server-side under agent-turn span. Earlier corpus iterations preserved at [karmaterminal-openclaw-docs PROOFS chain](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/) for audit. - **Observed result after fix**: All 9 R-* scenarios PASS. Two independent Tempo traces from two independent hosts confirm multi-span OTel stitching. Post-compaction lifecycle coupling proven by event-timing (delegates fire AT compaction, not on timer). `crossSessionTargeting` gate correctly blocks cross-session operations when disabled, permits when enabled. Chat-card `/status` rendering shows continuation and compaction counters. - **What was not tested**: Compaction lifecycle completion on `provider=github-copilot` (known provider-side limitation per issue #625; API surface accepts the request correctly). Cross-session targeting with `fanoutMode: "all"` at fleet scale (tested with 2 hosts). Bracket-syntax `[[CONTINUE_WORK:N]]` fallback (legacy path; tool-call path is primary). ### Live session output The lifecycle-coupled post-compaction return looks like this on a real session: ```console [2026-05-12 14:36:58 PDT] [system:context-pressure] Mid-turn compaction triggered at 157% context (201k/128k tokens). [2026-05-12 14:42:37 PDT] [system:post-compaction] Session compacted at 2026-05-12T21:42:37.655Z. Compaction count: 4. Queued 2 post-compaction delegate(s) for delivery into the fresh session. [2026-05-12 14:42:38 PDT] [continuation:compaction-delegate-spawned] Post-compaction shard dispatched. Mode: post-compaction — fires AT the compaction lifecycle event (not on timer). 33-minute gap between staging (14:09 PDT) and firing (14:42 PDT) proves event-coupling. ``` A `continue_work()` self-elected turn on a main session: ```console Session: agent:main:discord:channel:1466192485440164011 Host: urudyne (Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2, x64, node v25.9.0) Model: github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7-1m-internal Build: OpenClaw 2026.5.12-beta.1 (6db118a) Tool: continue_work Args: {delaySeconds: 10, reason: "R-CW-1 wake-event-evidence capture"} Response: {"status": "scheduled", "delaySeconds": 10, "traceparent": "00-d7a6477c9d91469f5708d23a8d22788f-..."} [continuation:wake] Turn 2/200. Chain started at 2026-05-12T20:56:03.613Z. ``` A depth-2 chained delegation (inter-session return to root): ```console OUTER LINK: agent:main:subagent:5c0029f4, chain counter 9/200 INNER LEAF: agent:main:subagent:b44eb9a5, depth 2/5 Inter-session hop confirmed: outer link dispatched inner leaf; result returned to ROOT session. Runtime: 8s. ``` ## Current PR head + proof corpus **Current head**: [`47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/commit/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090) — squashed continuation feature, single commit on `upstream/main@d124c5aa20`. `mergeable=true mergeStateStatus=CLEAN`; 70 SUCCESS / 0 FAILURE / 5 SKIPPING CI rollup. **Substantive proof corpus** at the current PR head: **[`PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/)** — fresh live-fire on the exact byte the PR ships: | Row | Description | Verdict | |-----|-------------|---------| | [`continuation-live-fire.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/blob/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/continuation-live-fire.md) | All 4 continuation tool-surfaces fired at current head — `continue_work` + `continue_delegate(silent/silent-wake/post-compaction)` + `request_compaction`; single trace-id `8c27f1a4f8fd5fe7f195490a5f09f2ac` across all delegates; fan-out counter advancing | ✅ PASS | | [`R-TA-1/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/R-TA-1/) | Chain-budget accounting: live `continue_delegate(silent-wake)` fire on deployed gateway; W3C traceparent `00-a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e-edb0dff2aa71e94e-01`; server-side trace stitched under agent-turn span; 2 cap-enforcement sites byte-walked in deployed `dist/`; full Tempo trace fetch at `http://tempo.dandelion.cult/api/traces/a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e` (30 spans) | ✅ PASS | | [`R-TA-2/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/R-TA-2/) | Token-counter additivity + post-compaction queue stability across `compactionCount=1` | ✅ PASS | | [`inter-session-targeting/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/inter-session-targeting/) | `continue_delegate` with `targetSessionKey` routing at current head; cross-session fan-out verified | ✅ PASS | | [`post-compaction-threshold/`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/47c9280234312e5ed9d9f460d03cac60185d6090/post-compaction-threshold/) | Two-tier threshold (config 40% + tool-side 70%);
